- How to make a horse a friend. One cowboy's partnership with horses: Kind: captions Language: en On the eastern slopes of the Rockies Jimmy Anderson is a bit of a legend. Jimmy's part of a new generation of cowboys, who've left the old idea of "breaking a horse" behind. He doesn't "break" horses. He "starts" them. Jimmy>>In the past, when we've talked about colt starting and colt breaking, that's what it was. They wanted to get a job done. Kind of just force everything onto this horse. They expected the horse to jump, they expected the horse to rear, they expected them to buck, because there was no relationship. Talk about breaking the spirit... and now it's almost the opposite of what we're wanting now. We want that spirit, we want that heart, we want that desire. Niobe>>I'm here to see Jimmy start a two year old colt called Shiver. Until now, Shiver's only seen humans from a distance. He's never been ridden. Jimmy>>Now what I'm going to do is start working myself a little bit closer to him. Niobe>>I've come for the week, but Jimmy figures he'll get a saddle on sooner than that. [horse neigh] That seems a little optimistic to me... Niobe>>Jimmy and Shiver aren't on speaking terms yet. Baby steps. Jimmy>> There... there we go. So, you'll hear me talk about two eyes. Niobe.. Uh huh. Jimmy>> When that horse can start to give me them two eyes, he also gives me his focus. Jimmy>> Nice. He really needs to get used to this. Niobe>>If he can't take that touch, he won't take a saddle. Jimmy>>No. We're getting him used to an external pressure. Tap this hind end and have him yield that hind end. Nice. Tap this hind end. There... nice. I'm going to come up here and see if I can't get some petting in there. Niobe>>Jimmy knows horses are prey animals. Dogs are always looking for supper, but horses are looking for safety. And that need is Jimmy's magic key. Jimmy>>When I look at horses that are spooky... if he's away from the herd, he doesn't have a leader. When you get that trust and that confidence and that relationship with a horse, I mean, really sky is the limit of what you can do. When I start a colt, it's never a competition. It's more of a partnership and a relationship that we can start to get working together. here now with the rope I'm going to move them hindquarters. Move those hindquarters. [sound of tarp hitting] Niobe>>How do you know how far to take it and when to stop? Jimmy>>You know, we're looking at the expression in his face. Looking at that tightness through his neck. He's telling me "Jim, you can do more" or "Jim, I'm getting too worried, I can't take this". So that's where I am just reading him and letting him tell me how much. And so when I can go through this with him not getting worried and just no big deal, that's when it's going to tell me, he's ready to saddle. Now he's getting good with the tarp. Good boy. Niobe>>So what are you doing now Jim? Jimmy>>I'm just bringing Maverick in. Maverick is going to give him a little bit of confidence as I put this saddle on. Niobe>>And that's the first time? Jimmy>>That's the first time right there, yeah. [bucking sound] Jimmy>>And that's totally OK with me. He kind of got worried. He had to deal with it that way a little bit, that's totally OK. Good... there he moved his feet. Kind of tightened up, but come back. And so the next thing that I'm working here... see every time he stops, I want him to start to look for me. [trotting sound] Jimmy>> There we go ... Nice! That's what I want to see. That focus, that looking to me for that answer. Niobe>>He's come a long way since morning. Jimmy>>He's come a long ways, yeah. [sound of rope hitting saddle] I need to get him used to me jumping up and down here, because if I want to get on him. Really, really good changes here. [Jimmy signals Maverick] Niobe>>Just amazing. Completely calm. Jimmy>> [clicking sound to Maverick] Jimmy>>My partner is helping me. [Niobe laughs] [Jimmy makes kissing noises] Jimmy>> Good! Nice! Niobe>>That was just superb! That was incredible to watch. Jimmy>>Good boy! You know, couldn't have asked for a better first day... good boy! And here he is for... first time. Niobe>>Yeah he's your best friend now, isn't he? Jimmy>>Yeah. When I take the halter off, what I want to see, is him not leaving me. [Jimmy whistles] His focus isn't there. Nice! Perfect. See and I want to be hooked up to these... not through a rope or through manhandling or forcing, through their mind. Niobe>>In a single morning, Jimmy and Shiver have found a common language. It shows us, horses judge humans. And some of us pass the test. Jimmy>>Horses are really amazing how much they will be a partner and they try a crazy amount and I want to try that same amount back for them. Maverick knows, I won't let something happen to him. ♪♪♪

- HOW TO CARE FOR A HORSE (Complete Guide): Kind: captions Language: en hey everyone so today i'm gonna give you a crash course on all the basic information you need in order to care for a horse so whether you're buying a horse for the first time or maybe you're just starting to get into lessons and you just like to know how you care for a horse this is a great place to start so if you are getting a new horse why don't you comment below and just let me know some more information about your horse and what you plan to do with them and as always be sure to like this video and subscribe to our channel [Music] so the first point we're going to talk about is what your horse eats so your horse may eat a few different things the main thing they're going to eat is grass so grass is a horse's natural diet in the wild so it's the thing that they need most of ideally your horse's main sustenance will come from grass and the reason that is is because there's lower sugar content compared to grain so it's better for them and they're made to be eating all day and so grass has that abundance to where they could be grazing all day as they need to so there are different types of grasses that your horse will eat and some grasses have more sugar than others that's just something if you want to know more you can research some horses can get super fat off of grass these horses are called easy keepers meaning they don't need that much sustenance in order to stay a healthy weight tucker here is an easy keeper he stays pretty much the same no matter what he's on so that's pretty nice so the next main thing your horse may eat is hay so hay is basically just dried grass and there are many different types of hay there's alfalfa hay timothy grass and the list goes on so you can sustain your horse's diet with hay we feed it in the winter just because the grass dies off and so the horses need that forage if you are stalling your horse or maybe your horse is injured so they have to be kept in a stall you will want to provide the horse with hay and this way they can just have that food because you know horses are used to eating all day and so hay gives them the opportunity to constantly be munching instead of just eating grain which they eat in you know five minutes and then they're done if you're going to be purchasing hay it's important that you purchase horse quality hay horses and cows are different so they can digest different things cows can pretty much eat anything and they're fine horses on the other hand there are a few things they can't digest as well as cows so you want to make sure you're getting horse quality hay we one time got hay and it unfortunately wasn't the best quality and it had this thing called fox tail in it and the fox tail is like a thistle almost and it completely ripped up tucker's mouth and so that's just one example of how you need the horse quality hay and you need to even check that horse quality hay for things like foxtail and other weeds that may damage your horse's mouth or not be good for them to digest so many people also like to feed their horses grain when it comes to feeding grain it's important to feed grain that will benefit your horse's diet and your horse's overall health so in my personal opinion not every horse needs grain grain can be high in sugar and it's just unnatural for the horse to digest because horses are used to getting natural sugars from grass compared to grain that has a high amount of sugar that being said some horses do need to sustain their weight with grain it just is up to really your horse is going to need in order to survive is water so horses need between 5 to 10 gallons of water a day so you want to make sure that they have enough water to get them through their day if can freeze even the water buckets kept installed so it's important that you make sure the water is staying unfrozen you can get water heaters for these or you can even try some things like there's a move as they need to and that they can graze the whole day and that they can eat hay and all that stuff if your horse is out 24 7 it's important that they have adequate shelter from the elements just them or something like that when it comes to deciding whether or not you want to keep your horse in the stall it's important to remember that a horse's natural habitat is out in the wild where they can roam so keeping them in a stall is kind of the opposite of what they're used to this is just important to remember because some horses can get stocked up legs if they're standing for too long in a stall or it can cause their joints to lock up so this is just a very important thing to be aware of and to know when it comes to deciding where you want to keep your horse you can always consult a vet - Top 7 Beginner Rider Habits to BREAK in 2024: Kind: captions Language: en everyone has bad habits biting your nails leaving the toilet seat up or mindlessly Doom scrolling social media but as motorcyclists being cognizant of your bad habits and making a concerted effort to mitigate them is crucial for Success because when riding a bike a bad habit won't just annoy your friends and family it can make the difference between a joyous ride or one destined to end in disaster having good riding habits is synchronous with having a proper Foundation upon which to build your repertoire of skills and to grow as a rider So today we're going to talk about seven beginner bike habits that are crucial to break let's get into it the first habit that is crucial for New Riders to break is improper use of the rear brake some Riders rely too heavily on the rear brake While others completely forget that it exists while it is true that your front brake is responsible for 70% of your motorcycle stopping power using the rear brake effectively can increase your overall braking ability and reduce the time it takes you to bring your motorcycle to a stop I'm a big Advocate that beginner Riders just focus on the front brake for now when riding on on the road rear brakes are nice for slow speed stuff and you can balance both of them in an emergency situation but truthfully stopping on the rear brake is a quick way to slide by using the rear brake in conjunction with the front you add stability to the motorcycle when the rear brake bites onto the brake rotor effectively slowing the rotation of the tire less momentum is forced onto the front of the motorcycle preventing front end dive and helping to keep the rear tire in contact with the pavement it's why when you come up to a stop sign you can blend front and rear brake and notice that if you do it correctly you basically get no front-end dive it's kind of cool try it out next time dragging the rear brake while slipping the clutch can all to operate your bike very smoothly at low speeds which is really helpful in parking lots and tight slow speed turns alternatively when Riders rely too heavily on the rear brake they're losing out on all of the stopping power that comes from the front this is more common amongst Cruiser riders or people that come from the dirt bike World Cruiser Riders have been known to disseminate a ton of antiquated and inaccurate information based solely on the vague anecdotes of someone who knows someone who did something one time for instance a cruiser Rider thinks that using the front brake in an emergency situation will immediately cause them to go down because someone they knew was riding out or raked out Harley and crashed after emergency breaking while trying to avoid a deer when in reality for a properly built motorcycle with normal geometry from the factory using the front brake and rear brake at the same time is best practice relying too heavily on the rear brake which cannot stop a motorcycle anywhere near as fast as the front which provides a majority of the stopping power increases your risk of locking up the back wheel as well because when exclusively using the rear braak the window of progressive braking is as follows not slowing down not slowing down not slowing down barely slowing down locked up beginners must learn to use both the front and the rear brake appropriately and in the correct situations but when in doubt the front brake is going to be your friend that's the one you should really be thinking about and be using especially in emergency situations a bad habit that many beginner Riders have is poor throttle control there are many downsides to having poor throttle control there is of course extreme examples like looping your B or losing control or whiskey throttling but those situations usually come from a complete lack of respect for the throttle and perhaps a splash of hubris but unless you weigh only 100 lb and you're riding something with over 120 horsepower it takes a lot of throttle input to have a bike just completely come out from underneath you that isn't to say it never happens or is completely impossible for a new Rider but it is an extreme outcome it is far more likely that a new Rider with bad throttle control are just going to have choppy and imprecise movements especially in low speed situations or in stop Ando traffic begin to familiarize yourself with your motorcycle's throttle response in a controlled environment some new fuel injected motorcycles can have odd quirks in the throttle response and power delivery low down so it's best to Fu these out in a lows speed situation as opposed to on a busy roadway when you start to feel comfortable with the throttle response you'll be able to more quickly increase or decrease your throttle input to change your speed and mastering your throttle goes hand in hand which using your clutch I mean literally hand in hand anyways many New Riders can have bad habits when using the clutch I recently saw this video of this guy that was riding and the shifting with the clutch was so atrociously awful it literally made me physically sick we're going to clip it here just for a little bit I just really want to show you guys this is what you don't want to do with the clutch some bad clutch habits can include not mastering the friction Zone a failure to fully engage the clutch when shifting unintentionally leaving the clutch partially engaged and coasting with the clutch pulled in using the clutch is imperative for slow speed Maneuvers where slipping the clutch in the friction Zone allows you to apply very minimal but smooth amounts of power to the rear wheel New Riders can be unfamiliar with this practice and have difficulty with low speed Maneuvers but if you have the clutch pulled in too far you won't be putting any power to the wheel and you won't move the bike if you have the clutch lever too far out you'll either risk stalling or move forward too quickly depending on your throttle input and having a strong familiarity with your clutch is also crucial when shifting gears if your clutch is not fully engaged when shifting gears your bike will shift roughly or not at all as well as put unnecessary wear on your transmission conversely accidentally leaving the clutch partially engaged for too long is going to put unnecessary wear on the clutch and reduce the power delivered to the rear excessive clutch wear can cause your clutch to slip under high power loads and will untimely require the replacement of clutch plates after shifting gears releasing the clutch too quickly can also lead to choppy power delivery and even possibly an unwanted wheelie but again kind of rare lastly a bad habit that New Riders have is to Coast with the clutch lever pulled in coasting like this prevents you from being able to make adjustments to your speed decreases your traction on the road and there's no force from the engine or brakes being put on the wheels and will not allow you to engine break the clutch should be used when changing gears and slipped using the friction Zone during low speeds otherwise the clutch lever remains untouched a bad habit that is super common amongst beginner Riders and really imperative to break for your safety on the road is having Target fixation ation Target fixation occurs when a motorcyclist looks at an obstacle that they're trying to avoid and unintentionally ends up steering right towards it when you're riding a bike it moves in the direction your body tells it to you look where you want to go it works every time and almost always whether intentional or not your body is going to direct your motorcycle in the direction of the object you're looking at that is why it's really important that you always look where you want to go by doing so your body is going to instinctually Usher your motorcycle in the right direction so if you stare at the pothole you're trying to avoid you're likely going to ride right into it Target fixation can be catastrophic in instances when you come into a turn too quick and fixate at the outside edge of the curve instead of the exit or even worse fix it on a tree a car or some other immovable object that you don't want to ride into this can be a tough habit to break as you instinctively want to stare at the thing you're trying to avoid but by doing so you're much more likely to ride right into it instead of avoiding it as you become aware of the moment you have Target fixation it becomes easier and easier to look instead where you want to go not where you're trying to avoid and remember while turning always look at the exit it's much easier to avoid Target fixation if you're riding within your limits if you're riding way outside of your limits or close to your limits you're way more likely to Target fix a it's also common for beginner Riders to fail to understand counter steering and instead be in the habit of trying to turn at speed only by leaning when controlling a motorcycle at slow speeds turning the handlebars moves the bike the way you would expect turning to the left will make the bike go left and turning to the right will make it always go right but as you increase the the speed the physics of your bike require counter steering to initiate lean and move the bike in the direction you wish to go but one thing to note here is that counter steering actually happens at all speeds if you think about it when you look at a bicycle the way it turns it always falls into the corner in the direction of the wheel opposite of the way it wants to go so counter steering works at all speeds it's just that it slow speeds it's a little less noticeable when moving at speed you're approaching a left- hand curve you will not pull the left hand grip towards you as you would when you're turning in a parking lot but instead push the left grip forward when you push the handlebar in the the direction you want to turn it causes a slight fall of the motorcycle in that direction initiating lean to make quick and control turns especially at higher speeds you must counter steer first but the thing is motorcycles can only turn by counter steering so you'll always counter steer once the bike starts to lean you can maintain the turn by adjusting your body position and using throttle control turning a motorcycle at high speeds is very different than turning at slow speeds and counter steering is an obvious and great skill to improve your maneuverability of your bike it becomes intuitive to experienc Riders but will take some practice for beginner as they start to break their old habits motorcycles are as safe as they've ever been there are countless constantly evolving safety features that can help keep Riders safe under hazardous situations and while a lot of this Tech was originally designed for top-of-the-line super bikes over the years it has trickled down into more beginner-friendly models as well and while rid raids like ABS traction control and adjustable ride modes are great for when you really need them there's always the threat that a beginner Rider who's only exposed to riding bikes with such fail safes may become overly Reliant instead of mastering proper throttle control or optimal braking Tech techniques for instance you'll often see Riders talk about how they started on a motorcycle that may not normally be advised for new Rider due to power output but they said they kept it in Rain mode for the first few months the issue being that because rain mode reduces the sensitivity of the throttle the rider is not actually learning proper throttle control but having the ECU do it for them often times these different ride modes all retain the same max power number but they just modulate the throttle sensitivity and the torque curve so a beginner that starts on a big bike in Rain mode is still going to have too much max power at their disposal and will ultimately be worse off when they take it out of rain mode where the throttle response is no longer neutered similarly a new Rider may take greater risks with their braking knowing that ABS will kick in should they be too aggressive with their braking in a way that risks locking up their wheels obviously ABS is awesome to have should you need it but it's still important for beginner Rider to master Progressive braking and try to avoid any chance of locking up their wheels even when riding on a bike with abs they're great when you need it but those Rider raats are no substitute for good riding habits and the last bad habit beginner riders need to break is riding without the proper gear I see it a lot yall want to look cool on Tik Tok and Instagram and here I am your papa yam on YouTube crusty as I am telling you to wear your gear but I'm going to say it once again beginner Riders are often trying to spend as little money as possible to start their motorcycling Journey so they think hey I'm just a beginner I'm not going to be going that fast or taking any crazy long rides anytime soon I can make do with just a helmet and gloves for now and get more gear later unfortunately many riders will be forced to learn learn this lesson the hard way no matter what speed you're going or how short of a ride you're taking there's always a chance of a crash whether it be due to poor riding habits an unforeseen obstacle or other vehicles on the road there are ways to mitigate risk on a motorcycle and one of the easiest and most Paramount ways to reduce the chance of severe bodily harm on a motorcycle is by wearing proper gear look motorcycles are as safe as they've ever been and gear is as safe as it's ever been standards materials manufacturing processes continue to elevate year-over-year and there's more options for affordable safety rated gear GE than there's ever been there's also gear for every riding style so you can find stuff that won't break the bank will match your ride and will be comfortable enough for everyday riding you don't have to look dorky or like you're wearing all the safety gear there's tons of stealth - 7 Habits New Motorcycle Riders Must Develop: Kind: captions Language: en any experienced motorcyclists can attest that controlling a motorcycle becomes automatic and instinctual years of practice set you up to be able to freely and safely ride your bike without expending too much brain power on what it is that you're actually doing you just ride and flow and your instincts keep you safe and in control that is why it is very important to develop good habits early on in your riding career so without further Ado here are 7 Habits New Riders must develop the first habit every motorcyclist must develop is to always be aware of your surroundings also known as riding as if you're invisible also known as riding as if every car on the road might try to kill you that is a bit of a broad statement so let's break it down a little bit if you're fresh out of your msf course you probably still have the echo of the expression ride like your invisible bouncing around in your Noggin and if your msf was anything like mine was your instructor just said ride like you're invisible over and over and then had you waddling around on a motorcycle in a parking lot like a toddler with a diaper full of freshly metabolized sized peas and carrots so what does riding like your invisible mean cars are big motorcycles are small car drivers are easily distracted by the car load of screaming goblins in the back seat the digital gateway to the entire universe in their pocket or the invasive thoughts of how truly unfulfilling their life actually is so the last thing on their radar is the person on a motorcycle in their blind spot beside them when you're on your motorcycle in traffic you should assume you are always in someone's blind spot and that other cars on the road do not recognize your existence just like your father on your fifth birthday when he was too busy betting all of your presents down at the dog track and if these cars can't see you you need to make sure you see them all of them not just the cars in your lane but the cars cross in the intersection ahead of you or the car is about to merge onto the freeway you can't assume that a car's smart features like blind spot detection will notice you because it seems most companies that manufacture automobile safety Tech features had a brain fart and forgot that cars aren't the only vehicles on the Road and didn't fine-tune these sensors to always recognize motorcycles as well I wanted to put this habit at the top as many other habits are extensions of this mentality another good habit to develop on your motorcycle is trying to always visualize multiple Escape paths in case a car decides to kill you are you catching on yet when you're riding on the road it is good to think about where you will go in the instance that the normal path straight ahead of you becomes impassable like when the brakes go out on a fire truck and sends it plummeting full speed into the roadway a few car lengths ahead of you will you a crash head first into it B jump over it in the empty car hauler that happens to be parked perfectly on the side of the road with its ramp down like you're in Grand Theft Auto or C speed up into the furthest Lane and pass the intersection before the fire truck crashes through Jokes Aside an accident or unforeseen situation can Arise at any moment while you're on your motorcycle and is good to get into the habit of knowing where you can safely ride to evade a dangerous situation this includes no knowing the best Lane position to be in being aware of the speed of the cars around you and detecting potential dangers before they become an emergency is there a Dodge Hellcat riding everyone's ass during rush hour where traffic can come to a screeching halt at any second if you find yourself in a situation like this make sure to see what possible path you can take if an emergency situation were to take place on the same topic it is a good idea to practice emergency braking in a safe space like a parking lot so you can determine how much pressure you'd be able to put on your brakes before they lock up especially if your motorcycle did not come equipped with ABS and the last tip relating to other people on the road is to make a habit of keeping yourself cool allowing yourself to get heated by other drivers acting egregiously is only going to put yourself further at risk when you're in a state of heightened emotions you are more likely to ride aggressively and potentially unsafely or beyond your means yet can be incredibly frustrating that a car cuts you off but just take a breath think about how you can avoid that situation in the future and move on if you get angry try to speed up and pass him or are too distracted shouting and giving some artistic hand gestures you're losing focus on what you're actually doing on the road and when push comes to shove if you put an angry car up against an angry motorcycle that car is gonna win every time so don't even try there is no sense in spending all this time practicing and learning how to be a good motorcyclist if the moment a car driver does Annoying car driver crap it all goes out the window because any new Rider will quickly learn that there is no shortage of opportunities for car drivers to show you how Reckless impatient selfish and straight up dumb that they are on the road so if Karen pulls out in front of you on her Warner Bros Edition Chevy minivan give her the benefit of the doubt and assume she couldn't see past the 10 different family stick figure decals she has on her window because it is a responsibility of motorcyclists to do the seeing and thinking for everyone on the road because car drivers just can't seem to be trusted to do so on their own now guys we have a super special giveaway bike coming later this week it is a truly legendary bike that I think you guys are gonna go bananas over can you guess what it is here's a hint other than the Triumph Daytona 675R it was my dream bike before I started riding motorcycles almost 10 years ago maybe it's the namesake of the channel anyways if you want to find out what our new modern classic giveaway is going to be head over to yamanube.com and become a member get 10 off on all purchases on our store and you'll also get access to the Discord server where you can get behind the scenes looks on everything that's going on here at yammy noob if you want to find out what the next giveaway bike is that's the way to do it head over to yamanube.com select one of our most popular bundles and join up speaking of seeing it's also important to stay looking ahead on a motorcycle this is an essential habit for multiple reasons firstly you need to be able to anticipate the road conditions is there traffic an accident a Dodge Hellcat tailgating every other car on the road wait did I already make that joke you need to be able to anticipate these situation by looking ahead secondly and just as important you need to look ahead when turning this is also something you are hopefully taught in your msf course but it is super important when writing if you aren't looking through a turn you won't have a good read of the angle of the turn and be able to judge appropriate lean angle or speed and you'll actually be fighting against your own subconscious your body and motorcycle will naturally follow in the direction that you are looking so if you are looking through the turn your body and motorcycle will be easier to guide through whereas if you're looking at the middle of the turn or at the edge of the road as you rapidly get closer and closer to the edge worrying that you misjudge your entry speed you are even more likely to ride right towards it a common cause of crashing during a turn is Target fixation like if you're in approaching a turn and you see a car waiting to enter the road after you pass and you look at this car instead of looking at the exit of the turn you're more likely to turn wide and end up sliding off right into the road or possibly right into the object that you wanted to avoid in the first place so when you're turning look where you want to go keep your eyes on the exit and your body is going to be far more likely to steer the bike to the right place the next habit that is important for New Riders is to be smooth and purposeful with the controls developing the proper finesse for motorcycle controls takes time and that is why it is recommended that New Riders begin on a beginner bike that is not too powerful as choppy or misguided operation of the motorcycle controls on a powerful bike can lead to Whiskey throttle where you eat yourself into infinity and you're not going to be able to fit into your furry costume for furry con if you're in a full body cast this can be in reference to the relationship between your clutch and throttle if you aren't smooth on either and haven't become well acquainted with fingering your bike's friction Zone you won't be able to smoothly direct power to the rear wheel too much throttle and then quickly releasing the clutch can cause you to wheelie or spin the back tire or lose track which sure can be fun if it's done intentionally or safely but it can really mess up your day if it happens when you're not expecting it or while the motorcycle isn't completely upright or in a straight line you also want your shifting to be smooth which requires a proper timing and manipulation of the clutch and throttle as you change up or down gears you also need to learn how to be smooth on the brakes especially in regards to emergency braking you do not I repeat do not want to be going 65 miles per hour and have your front brake lock up because there's an old lady in a Babushka shuffling across the road with her walker 20 years ahead of you you'll want to learn to progressively add pressure to your brake so you can stop safely and effectively it's also imperative to ride the back brake during slow speed Maneuvers if you're really good at working the friction Zone and riding the back brake you can inch along so slowly you can practically track stand your motorcycle which means you won't have to water your bike around parking lots like a total Noob it's a lot easier to be smooth on the throttle if you're not holding yourself up with all of your weight on your hands this habit is pretty specific to sport bike riding but that is holding yourself up with your legs by squeezing the tank one of the earliest mods a lot of sport bike riders do is install tank grips on their motorcycle so they can easily grab under the tank with their legs and keep their weight off their hands holding yourself in position by squeezing your tank makes it so much easier to have smooth control of your motorcycle by keeping weight off your hands you're able to make much smaller and purposeful movements that way if you run into some bumpy roads or ride your R6 on some single track you won't be accidentally moving the throttle as you soak up the bumps with your arms similarly it is a good habit to stop holding onto the throttle all ham fisted like a Harley Rider holds onto a beer bottle and instead hold on to it like you would hold a screwdriver this allows for improved throttle articulation and less herky-jerky power delivery and the last good habit to develop on a motorcycle is to familiarize yourself with the bike and how to tell if anything is a miss or out of the ordinary this means regularly giving your bike a once over to check the brakes lights tire pressure and tired drift and fluids it is not uncommon for a lot of people to neglect their car until they're given a warning light or an outright just dies and they can Coast it off to the side to the road or they call AAA and have it taken to a shop whereas if something go seriously a Miss on your motorcycle you're a lot more likely to crash than if you have a car experiencing mechanical failures so also become familiar with your bike and the way it sounds and be able to recognize you should probably pull over and check everything out instead of just turning up the radio to drown out the bad sounds like car drivers do I think it's important to make New Riders aware that it is far more important to maintain their motorcycles than they have previously done on a car because if something goes wrong on a bike like blowing out a tire on the freeway or having a sudden loss of power you're far more likely to get hurt than if the same thing would happen in a car then you would have a motorcycle repair Bill and a hospital bill thanks for making it to the end of the video be sure to subscribe for more new Rider insights fact there are over 200 artificial languages that have been invented for books television and movies goodbye - Top Tips For Beginner Motorcycle Riders: Kind: captions Language: en All right, everyone. Here's an extremely useful tip for all riders, but especially new riders. So, whenever you're doing anything with the clutch, if you want something dramatic to happen, if you want to lose control of your bike pretty quickly, you want the bike to do something unpredictable, you just want the bike to behave in a way at which you don't want it to, start releasing the clutch like this. Just letting go of it. Ease out the clutch. So, like 1 second, like ease out. Whenever you're shifting gears, whenever you're taking off, whenever you're coming to a stop, like just be easing in and out. You don't have to go super slow motion like this. Like that's too slow, right? But, you don't want to let go. So, just like 1 1,000. Whenever you're shifting gears or doing anything with the clutch, just slowly in slowly out. You don't have to do anything crazy. The bike will be very predictable. It'll never freak you out. Your chances of stalling the bike is going to be extremely low, especially if you're a new rider. Just ease out the clutch. Nothing fast. Slow your hands down. Same thing with the throttle. Whenever you roll on the throttle, just 5 10 20 25 30 type You don't want to go 0 to 100. You want the bike to respond very quickly, very abruptly, and kind of freak you out, maybe whiskey throttle. Ah. So, start off first, flat wrist. Don't start like this because you're able to give it way too much gas and it'll pull you back and then give you more gas and more pull you back and now you're whiskey throttle right into the wall. So, start off with the low wrist. That's the first tip, not high. Don't ride around like this. Start off with the low flat wrist and just slowly on and slowly off the throttle. On and off. Nothing crazy, nothing twitchy. This is what happens. This is how people ride. Like, "Oh, my bike is really twitchy. This bike is really sensitive." No, you're moving your throttle way too fast. Slowly on, slowly off, like a hill. Nice and easy. Stop riding around like a heartbeat. Next, the brakes. Same thing. You want something dramatic to happen very quickly, start going like Hard on the brakes, grabbing the brakes, and then also letting go of the brakes. Don't do that. Reach up, 0% throttle, squeeze the brakes, and unsqueeze the brakes. Back to the throttle, accelerate, roll off the throttle, reach up, squeeze, and unsqueeze. Again, especially for new riders, you're in the beginner course, you're just starting to learn how to ride, you got your first bike, you're riding around. If you just slow your hands down, you'll be a very happy rider. The bike will be predictable, easier to ride, and it won't freak you out. Just slow your hands down. Everything else will be so much easier if you do this simple thing.

- How To Handle Team Members with Bad Attitudes - 6 Tried & Tested Steps: Kind: captions Language: en how to handle team members with bad attitudes disruptive team members is a problem we've all faced either when managing a team ourselves or working in a team other team members bad attitudes can quickly affect your own attitude and performance and the team's attitude and performance and dealing with negative employees is energy draining at the best of times for all those around them i'm going to take you through six steps to take to improve this situation as a manager of that team and if you are a co-worker these steps will be just as useful for you to follow as well this is what we're going to cover bad attitudes can be more disruptive than poor performance on teen dynamics happiness and overall team performance it is in no one's interest to allow team members to continue with a bad attitude [Music] my name is jess coles and i've had a 25-year management career in innocent drinks fosters ey peer consulting and many other less well-known companies i've also won best team prizes at national and company level i've had to learn how to handle team members with bad attitudes early in my career the worst thing to do is do nothing even though ignoring the problem appears to be the easiest like many new managers i learnt this the hard way if you're new to this channel enhance.training provides online business courses to help you improve your personal performance and that of your team and business and if you like this video please give it a thumbs up and subscribe so let's go through six key steps to take for the benefit of all when you're thinking about how to handle team members with bad attitudes the first and most important step is not to ignore the problem a team member with a bad attitude can quickly infect others with negative behaviors and you can easily see team morale and motivation heading downwards it is your job as a manager to handle this problem to do your job you cannot ignore the situation you must take action all the other team members are looking at you to see how you will react and your reactions are going to be setting the rules not what you say if you take no action you are effectively signaling to the team that it is perfectly fine to have bad attitudes through your lack of action if team motivation and happiness drop then team performance is likely to follow fairly soon afterwards and then you'll have your manager worried about your performance so take action as quickly as practical after you become aware that a person in the team has a bad attitude and if you're not the manager of the team then your role is to make the manager aware of the bad attitude of a team member and before you speak to your manager take the time to gather some examples note down what is said by the person and when start the conversation with your manager along the lines of peter i'm worried that something is wrong with bob he seems very down and is being very negative at the moment some of the examples of what he is saying to colleagues is and then go on to a couple of your examples if your manager is not aware of the problem they will not be able to take action and successfully manage disruptive employees so don't ignore the problem or hope somebody else will say something take the second step when planning how to handle team members with bad attitudes is to find out exactly why the employee has a bad attitude there can be huge range of reasons and until you know why the behavior is happening it is very hard to decide what action to take when planning how to handle employees with bad attitudes you know some examples of the reasons could be firstly the team member is really upset at how they're being treated at work colleagues might be causing them problems they might have a problem with what you as their manager is doing or the actions that you've taken or they may have recently missed a promotion etc there can be a whole range of reasons secondly the team member has problems at home or in their personal lives which is impacting their outlook and behavior at work third the employee is a negative or a glass half full person you know they're always looking for faults or what could go wrong fourth the employee might have you know low self-esteem or low confidence and is trying to bring others down to make themselves feel better these are only some of the many reasons before you start dealing with the negative employee speak to other team members you know is the bad attitude being experienced by all or many of the team members or is it just being experienced by one person collate a number of different examples from several team members so you can provide examples to the individual rather than just your opinion this creates a much stronger impact and is much harder for the team member with a bad attitude to just brush off then have a private meeting with the individual with a bad attitude use the examples to highlight the issues and ask what is causing this behavior then stop talking and actively listen to their answer give them plenty of space and time to respond don't take the next steps until you understand what is causing the bad attitude and disruptive behavior the third step when planning how to is to decide if the bad attitude is likely to be temporary or a more permanent feature for example if you have a team member who's worked at the company for years and until recently has had a good or reasonable attitude then there is a good chance of being able to improve their attitude if you have a new starter and they seem to be just selling discord within their team already then there is a good chance this might be a personality issue and a more permanent aspect to their behaviour and personality so after understanding the reasons for the bad attitude use your judgment to weigh up the effort of supporting the person to change their attitude and the likelihood of that support being successful against the time and effort you'll have to put in for what appears to be a temporary bad attitude then the effort is like to be very worthwhile if it appears a more permanent feature of their approach and behaviour providing support and coaching is a lot less likely to be successful you will not be able to change the behavior and attitude of the other person unless they understand the problem their bad attitude is causing and they want to change their approach the disruptive employee has to choose to change you can't make them change or choose for them the fourth step when planning how to is to decide the best course of action there are a wide range of actions that you could consider and it is important you choose the right approach for the given situation the surer you are that the individual is having a temporary period of bad attitude then the more it is in yours and the company's interests to support them to get back to a more constructive and positive attitude this saves the time and cost of hiring a new person and training them etc plus you're going to build loyalty in your team and with that person all good reasons for investing the time to see if you can help them change their attitude the more permanent the bad attitude appears to be or the less evidence that the person has been more positive in the past then the stronger i advise you to take steps to remove this person from the team and the company you know for example if the person has recently joined and is demonstrating a bad attitude when they should be on their best behavior what will they be like when they are more established in the business you know why take the risk and keep them in the business use your judgment as to how temporary or permanent the bad attitude is consider this when planning what course of action to take the fifth step when planning how to is to set out your expectations in writing in most scenarios i would suggest that you set out a personal improvement plan with the individual this is a written document that you put together setting out your expectations of their behaviour with examples of what good behaviour or attitude looks like a very important step is to get the individual to contribute and give them the opportunity to negotiate points that they feel are unfair or unreasonable if you have gathered sufficient examples in step two then this process will get to mutually agreed goals that you need by improving their behavior and attitude even if the individual is being difficult give the individual a time frame to improve their attitude you know three months is long enough to see significant improvement to a bad attitude if there is no or little improvement you can always take more formal actions sooner than three months set measurable milestones where possible so both you and the other party know when they have reached acceptable attitudes and behaviors this step is not as easy to do for attitude issues as it is before for performance issues an example might be you know team members no longer experiencing negative behavior and to measure this you could use several people's opinions rather than just your own you know this is fairer and is seen to be fairer and therefore less disputable then set up fortnightly or monthly progress meetings and capturing writing the progress or the lack of it with the individual try to avoid a situation which has your opinion pitted against theirs use factual evidence or third-party opinion as much as possible coach and support the individual as much as possible or get another person to undertake this role assuming you've decided that improving the individual's attitude is beneficial for the team and the business this is a really important step so do put the time and effort to do this the sixth step when planning how to is to make the change happen as a manager or leader learning how to manage disruptive employees to a successful conclusion is important as discussed bad attitudes can create a lot of problems for you the team and the business so one way or another you need to help the person's attitude to a much more positive place or move the person from the team and the i believe in giving team members a good chance spending time coaching listening and supporting them can make a huge difference to their attitude helping them remove the problem that is causing their bad attitude will also make a huge difference with valued employees that have added a lot to the team then making the time to support them is a no-brainer keep a documented framework of the support you provide and keep monitoring their progress against agreed expectations if you have listened supported and coached yet there is no or very little change to the team members bad attitude then you need to take sensible steps to remove them from your team before they do further damage or if the team member with a bad attitude is a new person on probation or early in their career with your company then it is your judgment call whether you move much more quickly to remove them from the business do not shy away from taking steps to remove disruptive employees with bad attitudes from your business good luck in how you go about dealing with negative employees by taking positive action you are signaling to the individual and your team that you will not put up with bad be as supportive and as fair as possible and if that doesn't work take alternative action remember the most important action to take in how to deal with an employee with a bad attitude is to start the process that i've taken you through the sooner the better do visit us and take a look at our online business courses and resources to help you lead and manage your team more effectively thanks for watching and i look forward to speaking to you again soon you

- How to Stop Fighting Intrusive or Negative Thoughts - Passengers on a Bus Exercise from ACT: Kind: captions Language: en Have you ever felt like your thoughts are running&nbsp; the show? Like, no matter how hard you try to push&nbsp;&nbsp; them away, they keep coming back louder than ever.&nbsp; Maybe you deal with intrusive thoughts that feel&nbsp;&nbsp; terrifying or you constantly overthink every&nbsp; decision or you beat yourself up with negative&nbsp;&nbsp; thoughts that tell you you're a waste of space.&nbsp; These thoughts can feel really overwhelming and&nbsp;&nbsp; can seem to stop you from moving forward in your&nbsp; life or from being happy or doing your work or&nbsp;&nbsp; building relationships. So if that sounds like&nbsp; you, then let me teach you an exercise that can&nbsp;&nbsp; help you break free from struggling with intrusive&nbsp; thoughts or overthinking. It's called the&nbsp;&nbsp; Passengers on the Bus Metaphor from Acceptance and&nbsp; Commitment Therapy. This metaphor was developed by&nbsp;&nbsp; Steven Hayes, the founder of ACT and it offers&nbsp; a simple but powerful shift. You are not your&nbsp;&nbsp; thoughts, you are the driver of your life. So&nbsp; imagine you're a bus driver. Everybody hold on!&nbsp;&nbsp; This bus represents your life and you&nbsp; get to decide where to go. Your job is&nbsp;&nbsp; to steer the bus toward your values. These&nbsp; are things like love, connection, growth,&nbsp;&nbsp; or purpose. But here's the catch. Just like&nbsp; your mind, your bus isn't empty. It's packed&nbsp;&nbsp; with passengers and these passengers are your&nbsp; thoughts. They're your emotions and they're&nbsp;&nbsp; your memories. Sometimes passengers get on and&nbsp; sometimes passengers get off. Right? Some of these&nbsp;&nbsp; passengers are friendly and encouraging. They say&nbsp; things like, "Thanks for picking me up!" "You're&nbsp;&nbsp; a great driver!" Right? Or you have thoughts&nbsp; like, I'm really grateful to have supportive&nbsp;&nbsp; friends and family or I'm getting better at&nbsp; things that used to be hard for me. But other&nbsp;&nbsp; thoughts are loud or critical or annoying or even&nbsp; threatening. They might yell things at you like,&nbsp;&nbsp; "You're a terrible driver!" "This is too scary!&nbsp; Turn back!" "You're never ever going to make&nbsp;&nbsp; it! So why even try?" Common intrusive thoughts&nbsp; include things like, what if I lose control and&nbsp;&nbsp; hurt someone? Did I say something offensive? What&nbsp; if my partner secretly doesn't love me? What if&nbsp;&nbsp; I'm a bad person? Okay. Let's imagine an example.&nbsp; Let's use Sarah. Sarah struggles with social&nbsp;&nbsp; anxiety. She values connection and meaningful&nbsp; friendships. But every time she thinks about going&nbsp;&nbsp; to a social event, passengers on her bus start&nbsp; yelling, "You're going to embarrass yourself!&nbsp;&nbsp; People will think you're awkward! You should just&nbsp; stay home where you're safe!" Now, in the past,&nbsp;&nbsp; Sarah listened to these passengers. She would&nbsp; cancel plans last minute. She would avoid meeting&nbsp;&nbsp; new people. She would distract herself with her&nbsp; phone. But this avoidance made her feel isolated,&nbsp;&nbsp; disconnected, and regretful. Now these thoughts&nbsp; can feel so real that they pull you off course.&nbsp;&nbsp; The passengers might demand that you stop the&nbsp; bus, that you take a detour, or even that you let&nbsp;&nbsp; them drive. These thoughts try to convince you&nbsp; that they're in control. But here's the power,&nbsp;&nbsp; they don't actually have the power to steer.&nbsp; Only you do, okay? Now on the other hand,&nbsp;&nbsp; sometimes we listen to these thoughts we believe&nbsp; them and we let them tell us what to do. But a lot&nbsp;&nbsp; of us also react to intrusive thoughts not&nbsp; by listening to them but by fighting them.&nbsp;&nbsp; So does your bus driver self ever argue with the&nbsp; passengers to try to prove them wrong? You might&nbsp;&nbsp; try anything to get rid of these thoughts, these&nbsp; passengers. You might yell at them and try and&nbsp;&nbsp; throw them off the bus. But to do that, you have&nbsp; to get up from your driver's seat and move away&nbsp;&nbsp; from driving in the direction you want to go. You&nbsp; might stop driving just to appease them. But the&nbsp;&nbsp; problem is the more you argue with these kinds&nbsp; of thoughts, the louder they get. For example,&nbsp;&nbsp; imagine a passenger might say, "Oh, you're a&nbsp; terrible person!" And if you start arguing,&nbsp;&nbsp; "No, I'm not! I do good things!" The&nbsp; passenger will probably remind you, "Oh,&nbsp;&nbsp; yeah? What about how you screwed up that&nbsp; thing at work yesterday?" And guess what?&nbsp;&nbsp; You're now sucked into a power struggle with&nbsp; your own mind. The more you fight the thought,&nbsp;&nbsp; the more power it has to hold you back. When we&nbsp; fight our thoughts, we usually lose track of where&nbsp;&nbsp; we were going in the first place. When we spend&nbsp; a lot of time trying to argue with our thoughts,&nbsp;&nbsp; then it generally doesn't help us live that rich,&nbsp; meaningful, purposeful life that we're trying to&nbsp;&nbsp; work towards with our day-to-day actions. Okay.&nbsp; So fighting with thoughts is just as harmful as&nbsp;&nbsp; listening to them. Oh, gosh! Okay. So you might&nbsp; wonder like but what if my thoughts are dangerous,&nbsp;&nbsp; right? Just like with this bus. Like, what if&nbsp; the passengers attack me if I don't listen to&nbsp;&nbsp; them? Shouldn't I force them off the bus? Okay.&nbsp; This is where we often stall out with intrusive&nbsp;&nbsp; thoughts. They seem so frightening that we get&nbsp; stuck trying to control them or even worse,&nbsp;&nbsp; we get stuck avoiding them. Okay. So intrusive&nbsp; thoughts like, "Oh my gosh! What if my partner&nbsp;&nbsp; doesn't love me? This is terrifying to me." Like,&nbsp; I have to check with them over and over and over&nbsp;&nbsp; again or you might have an intrusive thought like,&nbsp; what have I hurt my baby? This is a really common&nbsp;&nbsp; intrusive thought that comes after moms have&nbsp; babies. Now the truth is unlike actual passengers&nbsp;&nbsp; in a bus, our thoughts cannot hurt us. So, the&nbsp; goal of this exercise is to help us build up the&nbsp;&nbsp; skills to notice the thoughts and just say, "Thank&nbsp; you brain for coming up with another thought. That&nbsp;&nbsp; one's not helpful." Right? So in Acceptance&nbsp; Commitment Therapy this is called diffusion,&nbsp;&nbsp; getting unstuck from thoughts instead of fighting&nbsp; them. Trying to get rid of uncomfortable thoughts&nbsp;&nbsp; feelings often leads to us spinning our wheels,&nbsp; getting distracted getting out of our driver's&nbsp;&nbsp; seat, and getting quite stuck. So Sarah, for&nbsp; example with the social anxiety, Sarah would spend&nbsp;&nbsp; a lot of time trying to force herself to feel&nbsp; confident. Yeah! I have to be confident! Well,&nbsp;&nbsp; what if I'm not confident enough? Well, I'm fine!&nbsp; I'm fine! It'll be great! Everyone's going to like&nbsp;&nbsp; me. But what about Bob? I'm not sure he likes you,&nbsp; right? She tried to force herself to think and&nbsp;&nbsp; feel confident before going to the party and if&nbsp; she couldn't change her thinking, which sometimes&nbsp;&nbsp; happened, she wouldn't go to the party. Now&nbsp; if you're busy arguing with your thoughts&nbsp;&nbsp; or debating with your thoughts or listening to&nbsp; your thoughts, you might stop making decisions.&nbsp;&nbsp; You might avoid risks. You might withdraw from&nbsp; relationships or just give up on the things that&nbsp;&nbsp; matter to you to keep the passengers quiet. So&nbsp; they're like, "Hey! Don't go that way! That might&nbsp;&nbsp; be dangerous! You're going the wrong direction!&nbsp; What if something bad happens?" And you're like,&nbsp;&nbsp; "Fine! I'm going to park my bus. I'm going to stop&nbsp; going the way I wanted to go. I'm just going to&nbsp;&nbsp; look at Netflix all day and try not to be bothered&nbsp; by my thoughts." Right? But by doing that, you're&nbsp;&nbsp; letting those intrusive thoughts or negative&nbsp; thoughts decide what you do with your life. You're&nbsp;&nbsp; no longer able to pay attention to where you want&nbsp; your bus to go because you're so wrapped up in&nbsp;&nbsp; trying to make the passengers be quiet or to try&nbsp; to convince them to change. We're going to stick&nbsp;&nbsp; with this exercise because with practice you're&nbsp; going to learn that the passengers' thoughts and&nbsp;&nbsp; emotions, they can't actually hurt you. They just&nbsp; can get really loud at times. Okay. So instead of&nbsp;&nbsp; stopping the bus to argue with the passengers&nbsp; or pulling over so that they don't yell at you,&nbsp;&nbsp; we're going to take a different approach. Instead&nbsp; of fighting the passengers, fighting our thoughts,&nbsp;&nbsp; trying to control them, what if rather than trying&nbsp; to kick them off or arguing, you just acknowledge&nbsp;&nbsp; their presence and you keep driving anyway. Okay.&nbsp; So this doesn't mean that you like the thoughts.&nbsp;&nbsp; It doesn't mean that you agree with them. It just&nbsp; means that you recognize that they don't have to&nbsp;&nbsp; stop you. So when a self-doubt passenger pipes up&nbsp; with, "I don't think you can pull this off!" You&nbsp;&nbsp; would say, "Thanks for your input but I'm still&nbsp; driving toward what matters to me." "What if your&nbsp;&nbsp; husband doesn't love you?" "You know what? I'm&nbsp; just going to keep investing in my relationship.&nbsp;&nbsp; Thanks for popping up brain but I don't need to&nbsp; listen to you." Okay. Some of these passengers&nbsp;&nbsp; might always be on your bus, that's okay. You&nbsp; don't need them to leave before you can move&nbsp;&nbsp; forward. The goal isn't to get rid of them, it's&nbsp; to drive toward your values and that's where a&nbsp;&nbsp; lot of times we get distracted. You have to pull&nbsp; the bus over, stop going where you want to go, and&nbsp;&nbsp; spend all your energy trying to get rid of them or&nbsp; you can choose, "You know what? I'm just going to&nbsp;&nbsp; keep working toward building up my relationships."&nbsp; Right? So here's how you can use this metaphor in&nbsp;&nbsp; your own life to manage intrusive thoughts,&nbsp; negative thoughts, really loud overthinking&nbsp;&nbsp; thoughts. Right? Step number one, identify your&nbsp; passengers. So take a minute, if you're struggling&nbsp;&nbsp; with negative or intrusive thoughts, take a moment&nbsp; to write down some of the thoughts, emotions,&nbsp;&nbsp; or memories that tend to show up and derail you.&nbsp; What do they say? What do they try to convince&nbsp;&nbsp; you of? They might say things like, "No one likes&nbsp; you! You're never good enough! You shouldn't try!&nbsp;&nbsp; You might fail!" Right? Okay. Step two we're going&nbsp; to name and externalize the passengers. So we're&nbsp;&nbsp; going to give each of these types of thoughts a&nbsp; name like, "Hey! You're the Worry Wart! And this&nbsp;&nbsp; one is the Criticizer!" Right? And you externalize&nbsp; them and by doing that by giving them a name,&nbsp;&nbsp; this can help you see that these thoughts aren't&nbsp; you. You are not your thoughts. They're just&nbsp;&nbsp; passengers on the bus. So I often am like, "Oh,&nbsp; hey! There's my perfectionist mind speaking up&nbsp;&nbsp; again! You're sure loud today. But I don't have&nbsp; to listen to you!" Okay. Step three, you're going&nbsp;&nbsp; to acknowledge that they're there but you don't&nbsp; have to engage with them. So when these thoughts&nbsp;&nbsp; show up, you can practice saying, "I hear you but&nbsp; I'm still driving!" You notice them but you don't&nbsp;&nbsp; argue with them. Honestly, this is a skill like,&nbsp; the physical skill of driving while people say&nbsp;&nbsp; stuff is like, how you like actually get anywhere&nbsp; in a car as a parent. You're like, "Okay, my kids&nbsp;&nbsp; are saying all kinds of things, I'm just going&nbsp; to focus on like driving." When your thoughts&nbsp;&nbsp; are really persistent, you can imagine creating&nbsp; a little bit of space for them by imagining them&nbsp;&nbsp; sitting in the back of the bus rather than&nbsp; shouting from the front or you could just try&nbsp;&nbsp; like speaking to your thoughts by saying something&nbsp; like, "Wow! Self-doubt, like you're really loud&nbsp;&nbsp; today! Looks like you need to go to the back of&nbsp; the bus!" But even if they don't listen to you,&nbsp;&nbsp; it doesn't matter because you're driving the bus.&nbsp; Over time as you move toward your values, you'll&nbsp;&nbsp; probably find that some passengers quiet down&nbsp; or that you simply don't pay as much attention&nbsp;&nbsp; to them. Step four, this is the most important&nbsp; part, you keep your hands on the wheel. Right?&nbsp;&nbsp; You remind yourself where you want to go. What&nbsp; do you actually care about? What are your values?&nbsp;&nbsp; What kind of life do you want to create? Values&nbsp; are not the same as a destination. So destination&nbsp;&nbsp; might be like an actual concrete goal like, I want&nbsp; to get married. But the value is like, I value&nbsp;&nbsp; connection. I value closeness. I value commitment.&nbsp; Right? So for example, I value speaking up for my&nbsp;&nbsp; beliefs. I value serving and loving others. I&nbsp; value hard work. Values are about knowing what&nbsp;&nbsp; direction you're going in. I am traveling north.&nbsp; Now sometimes people wonder, "But what if I still&nbsp;&nbsp; have intrusive thoughts when I'm traveling north?&nbsp; What if the passengers keep yelling at me to turn&nbsp;&nbsp; around? What if my thoughts keep questioning my&nbsp; abilities? Shouldn't they be quieter?" Here's&nbsp;&nbsp; my answer: they don't matter. They're not the&nbsp; ones driving. Thoughts may seem to threaten&nbsp;&nbsp; you or others but as you practice defusion, as you&nbsp; practice this ACT skill of noticing your thoughts&nbsp;&nbsp; without listening to them or believing them or&nbsp; fighting with them, you'll learn that thoughts&nbsp;&nbsp; can't actually hurt you. When you focus your&nbsp; energy and attention on what you can control, they&nbsp;&nbsp; just won't matter as much. They'll probably quiet&nbsp; down. So no matter how loud the thoughts get,&nbsp;&nbsp; you keep driving in the direction that aligns with&nbsp; your goals. Okay. So back to Sarah. One day Sarah&nbsp;&nbsp; decides to handle things differently. She reminds&nbsp; herself, "I'm the driver of this bus. My values,&nbsp;&nbsp; not my fears, decide where to go." Her value is&nbsp; connection and that means showing up even if it's&nbsp;&nbsp; uncomfortable. So she goes to the party and the&nbsp; anxious passengers are still there. They whisper,&nbsp;&nbsp; "You're saying the wrong thing! Just leave!" But&nbsp; instead of arguing or trying to push them off,&nbsp;&nbsp; Sarah acknowledges them I hear you but I don't&nbsp; have to listen to you or fight you. I'm choosing&nbsp;&nbsp; connection. So she stays. She engages in small&nbsp; talk. She even enjoys herself and over time she&nbsp;&nbsp; realizes something. The more she drives toward her&nbsp; values, the less power the passengers have. They&nbsp;&nbsp; might come. They might go. Sometimes they're loud.&nbsp; Generally, they're quieter and most importantly&nbsp;&nbsp; they don't get to steer anymore. She starts to&nbsp; build better and deeper friendships and she starts&nbsp;&nbsp; to feel more comfortable being around people.&nbsp; Sometimes those passengers still yell at her. "Oh,&nbsp;&nbsp; my gosh! I can't believe you said that yesterday!"&nbsp; Right? But she just doesn't listen to them as&nbsp;&nbsp; much. This is how the Passengers on the Bus&nbsp; Exercise can help with social anxiety. You don't&nbsp;&nbsp; have to silence the fear. You just have to keep&nbsp; driving towards what matters and really the way&nbsp;&nbsp; our brains work is when we behaviorally act in&nbsp; a courageous way, it teaches our brain we don't&nbsp;&nbsp; have to be so scared and anxious anymore. Okay.&nbsp; Final thoughts. Maybe instead of anxiety, you're&nbsp;&nbsp; struggling with negative self-talk or emotion&nbsp; regulation or setting healthy boundaries. I have&nbsp;&nbsp; posted a lot of videos about those topics but I&nbsp; really like the Passengers on the Bus Metaphor as&nbsp;&nbsp; a tool for improving any of those things because&nbsp; when you see yourself as the driver, it's a really&nbsp;&nbsp; powerful way to shift your relationship with your&nbsp; thoughts and feelings. You really can coexist&nbsp;&nbsp; with those thoughts and those emotions or those&nbsp; physical sensations without letting them take&nbsp;&nbsp; control and this is the essence of psychological&nbsp; flexibility. You don't have to control all your&nbsp;&nbsp; thoughts to live a full and meaningful life, you&nbsp; just have to keep driving in the direction that&nbsp;&nbsp; matters to you. Okay, if this video was helpful&nbsp; for you and you'd like to get more tools for&nbsp;&nbsp; your mental health, you could sign up for my&nbsp; newsletter. Every week, I share a couple of&nbsp;&nbsp; videos and resources that can help you get better&nbsp; at feeling and the next time a negative thought&nbsp;&nbsp; or uncomfortable emotion tries to hijack you to&nbsp; your journey, remind yourself, "I'm the driver!&nbsp;&nbsp; The passengers don't get to decide where I go!"&nbsp; And then keep driving. Okay. Thanks for watching&nbsp;&nbsp; and take care. Hey! - How to Deal With Underperforming Team Members -Tried & Tested Approach: Kind: captions Language: en working out how to deal with an underperforming team member and then taking the right action is one of the bigger personal challenges any manager or leader faces at work i never liked dealing with underperformers yet i knew that procrastinating and doing nothing was the worst option i also learned that when dealing with underperformers as a manager you're very much on your own it is you that has to assess the situation have the difficult conversations decide on the action that needs to be taken and putting the time to deal with the situation your manager hr and official procedures etc usually offer limited support at best i'd like to take you through the approach that i've used successfully time and time again over the 25 years of managing teams there are six main steps or actions to take and towards the end of the video i share an example of improvement plans that i've successfully used in the past to show you how to document and monitor progress [Music] my name is jess coles and i've had a 25-year management career in innocent drinks fosters ey peer consulting and many other less well-known companies i had to learn what to do with a poor performer at work early in my career and i've successfully dealt with many underperforming employees since of the work i've done with underperforming employees i would say around 7 out of 10 have gone on to improve their performance and caught up with their peer group and i've had a few of these people go on to become star performers of the team i truly believe that helping an underperforming employee improve is the best result for all for them for you and the company if you're new to this channel enhance.training provides online business courses to help you improve personal performance and that of the team and business and if you like this video please give it a thumbs up and subscribe so the first must-do
