- Reality of India's Workers! | Dhruv Rathee: Kind: captions Language: en Hello, friends! Whether it's a 45°C hot summer day or there's water-logging outside due to heavy rains, whether there's a storm or there is too much pollution, whether it's day or night, today, in this era of convenience, we don't have to worry ourselves. Because we can remain in the comfort of our home, to order whatever we want, merely through a smartphone app. But have you ever thought about those strangers who make this possible? Have you ever thought about things from their perspective? These people are called Gig Workers. According to a survey by the National Council for Applied Economic Research, on average, a gig worker works for 69.3 hours in a week in our country. It's almost as if you work seven days a week, for almost 10 hours each day. Without any day-offs or Sundays. For comparison, in this survey, the other workers work an average of 56 hours per week. "Our seniors hand us the couriers, and give us all their headaches, we have to ride our bikes for 8-12 hours." The number of hours an average gig worker works for, the level of his qualification, and the little money he makes, hardly any other workers in our cities suffer as much. The amazing thing is that the qualifications of an average gig worker are better compared to other workers'. But at the same time, 75% of gig workers in our country are facing financial difficulties. On average, they earn only ₹18,000 per month, even after working hard tirelessly. In today's video, friends, come let's understand this gig worker profession in depth. Friends, the word 'Gig', G-I-G, means a temporary job or a performance act. Before modern-day gig workers became popular, usually musicians and comedians used this term. Because the job of these performing artists is such that they don't get a regular salary. They get paid for individual acts. If a comedian does a show in a company, it becomes a gig for him, for which he gets paid. The first time the word 'Gig' was used for a temporary paid job in 1952 when an influential author Jack Kerouac wrote an article about how he got a gig to work as a brakeman on a railroad. Today, freelancing is also included in this category and in the last 10 years, in fact, in the last 4 years, after COVID, we have seen a tremendous growth in the Gig Economy. Now, the Gig Economy can be divided into two parts. One is service-based gigs and the other is knowledge-based gigs. Services are offered by low to semi-skilled workers like the delivery agents. And knowledge is offered in high-skilled jobs like consultants and data scientists. Service workers are also called Blue-Collar Gig Workers and knowledge workers are also called White-Collar Gig Workers. But usually, whenever we talk about gig workers, it is the first category that is referred to. Those people who work for companies like Uber, Ola, Zomato, Swiggy, Urban Company, Porter, Zepto. Their work is different from desk jobs. And those people who are delivering these digital platform services in real life. Now, theoretically, if we see, in comparison to normal jobs, there are many benefits of gig economy for the employees and employers both. What are the benefits for the employers and the companies? They can hire the employees as needed, and fire them whenever they want. There is no contract with the employee. If they don't like an employee, he can be immediately removed from the job. And if they like someone, they can hire them immediately. On the other hand, the employees get flexibility. They can work when they want to. They can work for the company they want. They don't need to go to any office. They can work from anywhere. If they don't like a job, they can leave it and find another one. It's even possible to work for multiple companies at the same time. Giving them an opportunity to earn extra money. On top of it, if you have a main job, you can do it as a side job to earn an extra, supplementary income. There are so many benefits to it. It sounds too good to be true. So you might ask, what is the problem? The problem is that these benefits are only theoretical. In reality, practically speaking, the situation is getting bad to worse. As per NITI Aayog's 2022 report, India's booming gig and platform economy, before the COVID pandemic, had around 3 million gig workers in the country. But by 2021, this number reached 7.7 million. And this sector is expected to grow so much that by 2030, this number will reach 23.5 million. The Oxford Internet Institute's Online Labour Index tells us that India's online labour market share is at 24%. This means that India is the #1 country in the world in this regard. What do we learn from this? This tells us that most people in our country do not consider the gig economy for supplementary extra income but as their main job. In fact, as per Ipsos Research's 2024 survey, for 88% of gig workers in the country, gig work is their primary source of income. On the other hand, look at this number. Flourish Ventures' September 2020 research, 6 months after the lockdown, 90% of gig workers said that their salaries have gone down. 47% of gig workers weren't able to manage their expenses without borrowing money. During Covid, we called them Covid Warriors, Frontline Warriors or even Lifelines. But most people came to this profession due to compulsion. The Logical Indian covered the story of a gig worker named Karan Singh. Karan lived in Delhi's Paschim Vihar and was a construction worker. When construction work was stopped due to Covid, he had no source to get food, water or ration. His wife and two daughters depended on him. And the burden of running the household kept increasing. Then a friend told him about gig work. And he decided to use their last savings to get a second-hand scooter on lease. And he became a delivery partner for a deliver platform. Being a frontline worker meant that he could deliver essential goods during the COVID lockdown. But soon after, it became his main profession. And today, he works for a bike-taxi service platform. There are many such stories in our country, where the gig economy saved people's livelihoods. Today, these companies refer to their gig workers by different names. Some bike-taxi company, use the word Captain. Some call them Experts. But most of the companies use the word Partner. Normally, when the word 'Partner' is used in a business, it means that the person will share your work, will work with you, and the profits and losses will be shared. But is this the case for gig workers? The scooter or bike that a gig worker needs to do his job, has to be bought with his own money. The cost of petrol used, comes out of their pockets. Take the classic example of Uber or Ola drivers. Suppose, a taxi driver drops you off at a place and at that location, he can't find another ride. To find a ride, he might need to travel 15-20 km, to a hotspot. So, to travel those 15-20 km, the cost of the petrol used, does the company pay 50% of it? Not at all. "Suppose I'm riding my bike, and that uses petrol worth ₹100, if I don't get any orders, who will pay the ₹50 of it? Will the company bear it? That's what being Partners mean." So the question is, what kind of a partnership is this? Actually, friends, the word 'Partner' is used to avoid legal problems for these companies. If those companies start calling them Employees, then there will be a lot of responsibilities on those companies. They will need to provide health insurance. In case of a work-related accident it will be the company's liability. And the responsibilities of paying tax and a minimum wage. According to Oxford Internet Institute's Fair Work Research Project 2022, 11 Indian platforms were studied. And not a single one of these platforms could prove that their gig workers get at least local living wage after covering all work-related costs. Centre for Labour Studies at the National Law School, Bengaluru and Montfort Social Institute conducted a joint study in which they focused on the OLA and Uber taxi drivers in Hyderabad. They found something shocking. that the money a driver earns after working for a day on average 40% of that earning is spent on petrol or diesel alone. And most drivers work for 12-14 hours a day on average. Meaning, after bearing all the expenses, and considering inflation, if they can earn only a minimum wage, then these gig workers start working extra hours. It is also worth noting how these companies define themselves. They call themselves Tech Aggregators, Mediators or Facilitators. They will never use the word Employer. That too will create legal problems if they become Employers and Employees. They'll have to bear many expenses, and will need to take care of their employees. They don't want to get into these fuss, so they call themselves tech aggregators. These companies often claim that they have revolutionised the market by removing the middlemen. But just think about it, friends. What have they actually done is they have become the middlemen. On every ride, delivery, or service, they charge 15% to 25% commission and so have become a tech-based middleman. And since we're talking about these tech companies, then it is important to know how their software and algorithms create more problems Frontline covered an interesting case of a gig worker named Ankur. He has two sons. He used to work 12-14 hours a day for at least 6 days a week. as a cleaner with Urban Company. He used to travel between Delhi and Gurgaon daily. And one day, he met with an accident. Because of this, the jobs and gigs scheduled for that had to be cancelled because he had to get himself treated. And the next day, when he checked his account, he found that Urban Company had permanently blocked his account. He was shocked to see the reason behind this. When he checked, the reason was clearly written. "For cancelling more than 5 jobs a month." It wasn't about 5 days, it was about only 5 jobs. Since he cancelled 5 gigs because of his accident, his account was permanently blocked. Many gig workers face this problem where because of some random reason, their account or their ID is banned by these apps. There may be many reasons behind this. If someone calls the company and files a complaint against you. Or your ratings decrease on the app. Or you took some off-days. Think about it, friends. When I told you about the benefits of the gig economy, the biggest benefit for employees was that they get flexibility. But if there are such rules where rejecting a certain number of jobs can get you removed or cancelled from the app, is there any flexibility? This is a form of slavery. Where you will have to work to a set extent, and if you fail to do it, you will be removed from the app and you lose your job. According to this article, workers of Urban Company claim that the workers should have a minimum rating of 4.7 and the response rate should be above 70%. Moreover, not more than 5 jobs should be cancelled in a month. By avoiding being defined as an Employer, these companies are benefiting, these companies then use various ways to exploit these gig workers. The same Frontline article gives us another example of how, at one point, Urban Company gave discounts to customers, by using money out of workers' payment. Last year, more than 200 dark stores run by Zomato-owned Blinkit in Delhi NCR were shut down because the 'Delivery Executives' were on strike. What was the reason behind this? When the Delivery Executives signed a contract with Blinkit, the fees mentioned in the contract was ₹50 per order. But later it was reduced to ₹25 per order. And now it was reduced even further to ₹15 per order with a small distance-based fee component. A big issue here is that these gig workers have no channels to raise complaints against the company. Last year, we saw a case where Blinkit's employees were beaten up in Delhi because they didn't have loose change. Look at this case from January 2023, where 23-year-old Mohammad Rizwan was attacked by the customer's dog while delivering a Swiggy order. To protect himself from the dog's attack, Rizwan fell from the third floor and lost his life. The customer paid ₹500,000 as compensation for this as out-of-court settlement, but no compensation was paid by Swiggy. The company claimed that Rizwan was using his brother's account for the deliveries. So, for this accident, Swiggy bore no liability. This was a rare case but we do see many such incidents where people shout at gig workers for any delay in delivery. If the workers face any issues, their only recourse is to use chatbots for help. "If platform-based gig workers have problems, in their execution of work, or in fact, even if the customers have an issue with the execution of an order, your only recourse is chatting with a bot on the platform. You're prompted with question, you have to choose between a set of answers, that the bot is providing. If you are lucky to be able to cross a stage of conversation with the bot, there will be a call that will be made to you. But you don't have a toll-free number to which you can reach out and make a call. you have to wait for the call to come to you." It's quite rare for these apps, to allow them to call a human representative for help. According to Fortune India, most companies have replaced human managers and are using Artificial Intelligence or algorithms, to address complaints. For a customer, these apps are well made. If you are using these apps to order something, you will have multiple ways to raise complaints, and often, you get your money back for bad orders. But for these gig workers, the system isn't helpful at all. This is why we see so many strikes. In February 2023, workers of Ola, Uber, and Rapido staged a strike in Guwahati. Because their earnings were decreasing while the commission rate of the companies kept on increasing. The same month, 2,500 Ola and Uber cab drivers protested in Hyderabad regarding the high commission rates charged by these companies. Two months later, in April 2023, Blinkit's rate cuts happened. But despite the strikes, the rates remained the same. Many people don't understand the rating system of these companies. People think that if an Uber or Ola driver has provided a satisfactory service, he should be given 3 or 4 stars. He will get 5 stars only when he provides you an excellent service. On the other hand, people deduct stars on minor issues. Like, if a driver had some problem in finding the place, or was late, or didn't have the exact change. If you do the same, then think about it from the gig workers' perspectives. For them, falling below 4.5 stars often means losing their job. How will they run their household? So the next time you give a rating, 5 stars should be the default. If there are minor problems, ignore them. Unless these workers make a big mistake. Apart from this, do thank them for their hard work and sincerity. Because they work hard despite harsh conditions, and that is truly praiseworthy. And if we want to solve these problems, the solution needs to be stricter laws. Like other countries, the traditional labour laws in India do not cover gig workers. And as I said, companies can find loopholes quite easily. By not calling themselves Employers nor the gig workers Employees. It was only in 2020 that the Indian government passed a Social Security Code which included a definition of Gig Worker. "A person who performs work or participates in work arrangements and earns from such activities outside of traditional employer-employee relationship." It's a loose definition. According to this Act, a gig worker will get many Social Security benefits like Life and Disability Cover, Accident Insurance, Health and Maternity benefits, and old age protection. It sounds good enough, but the biggest problem is that this law has only been drafted. It hasn't been operationalised yet. Secondly, this law has a different definition of Gig Workers Platform workers, and Unorganised Workers. Platform workers are those who work outside the traditional employer-employee relationship. but they access organisations or individuals through an online platform and provide services for payment. An Uber or Ola driver can technically be called a Gig Worker as well as a Platform Worker. Because there is so much overlap in these definitions, it remains unclear how these schemes will apply to these workers. So, on one hand, where they are waiting for the Central Government to implement its Social Security Code, on the other hand, some state governments have started taking initiative to protect the rights of gig workers. Rajasthan government was the first to do so in 2023 before their state elections. The Rajasthan Platform Based Gig Workers (Registration and Welfare) Act. This Bill contains five main things. First, gig workers should be registered with the state government. Second, have access to social security schemes. Third, they should have a grievance redressal mechanism. Fourth, Welfare Boards be established and funded. For which the state government gave ₹2 billion. And fifth, there should be penalties for non-compliance by companies. The extra funds required to implement these, would come out of an additional cess of 1%-2% of the total bill, on every platform-based transaction. And because everything is carried out digitally, everyone will know exactly how did the work done by a worker, contributed to that fund. The Social Security benefits proposed to be given to the workers include accident, health insurance, maternity, gratuity, pension, EPF, ESIC, and even scholarships. It sounds really good. But how well is this law being implemented in Rajasthan? The relevant information isn't available online. Only those people can tell us this who are working in Rajasthan as gig workers. If you are one of such people, tell us more about this in the comments below. After Rajasthan, since Congress is in power in Karnataka too, a similar law has been proposed there a few weeks ago. The Karnataka Platform Social Security and Welfare Bill, 2024. And do you know what's interesting here? These companies have already started protesting against this bill. National Association of Software and Service Companies have claimed that the bill will harm the aggregators' business. Allegedly, this bill provides for a minimum notice period for terminations. Algorithmic disclosures are also being discussed. It wants to monitor and track different mechanisms. So some companies say that it will have a negative impact on their platforms and they won't be able to operate properly in the state. Think about it, these companies have a problem with giving minimum notice period to gig workers. The problems I listed in this video, other countries are also facing the same problems. And the solutions I talked about, these solutions have already been implemented abroad. The gig workers in Thailand and Malaysia get health and accident insurance which is financed by a similar 2% Cess on every ride. In America, the National Labour Relations Board has encouraged Uber and Lyft drivers and other gig workers to be organised and form labour unions. The UK and the Netherlands have recognised that gig workers have been misclassified. They are taking steps to reclassify them as employees. In December 2023, the European Union passed a Platform Workers Directive. According to this, all gig workers in European countries will be given rights and protections. Those who have been misclassified as being self-employed or a freelancer will be assumed to be regular employees. To do this, they created 5 criteria. There are five different criteria. If 2 of these criteria are satisfied, then a gig worker will be considered an employee. And the burden of proof is placed on these companies in relation to all gig workers working with the company, it is the company's responsibility to prove that these 5 criteria are not being satisfied. Now that this video is already so long, I won't go into the legal and technical details. Let's leave that to the lawyers and the experts who are working to fight for the rights of gig workers. Kunal Kamra has made an excellent documentary explaining this problem in detail. I will put the link to this documentary in the description below. Many clips used in this video were taken from this documentary. And finally, what can you and I do? Just as you open the door to your house to receive your orders, please, open the door to your hearts. The journey between Order Placed and Your Order Has Arrived try to visualise it. By default, if the transaction was satisfactory, then give them a 5-star rating always. And if there is a problem with your order, then remember that it is much better for you to complain as compared to telling the person delivering it. Link to Thatware is in the description below. And if you liked this video, then a stark contrast to this video, is this one. Where I talked about celebrities' fake lives. You can click here to watch it. Thank you very much!

- The Hidden Lives of Food Delivery Riders: Kind: captions Language: en-GB It's a stressful job. It's also quite hectic. You get tired after a while. Europe, where people come from far and wide with dreams. I want to own a shops, small businesses. They come with hopes and expectations. We have some dreams of our own when we come here and leave everything behind. If I wasn't delivering, I'd go out with a friend. But somewhere along the way those dreams quietly turn into survival. Normally, I work 10 hours. At least 12 to 10 hours. If you don’t earn… how will you survive It covers your expenses, there's nothing else in it. It's just work. Sometimes it's good, sometimes it's bad. Do they know you are a delivery rider? No, they don't. This is the story of those who despite being an important part of society, are overlooked . The average UK consumer spends a significant portion of their income on takeaway. But have you ever wondered what the lives of the people who run this entire system are like? In the eyes of our own people, we delivery drivers mean nothing. I dream of building a home of my own, on my own. buy a car someday. My work 8 hours of working. I make nearly £70 to £80 which is enough . Life here is so fast and busy that people don't have time to cook. Sometimes it's sunny, sometimes it's cloudy, sometimes it's raining, sometimes it 's stormy. But we keep going. According to statistics, approximately two out of every five people in Europe regularly use food apps. I spent a week with these riders and only then did I understand what their lives are really like. This work isn't as easy as it seems. it's not just riding. I mean, it's not just about riding a bike, there's a lot of waiting involved. You have to wait for your first order. Once the order arrives, you have to go to the restaurant and wait for it to be prepared. Once it's ready, you have to come back and wait there to make sure there's no problem. They'll come to get the order or not. I went to meet a Pakistani immigrant living in London who, besides being a mother, also works as a delivery rider in London. Mariam. Nice to meet you, Mariam. How are you? How can I come in? Mariam runs her own small business during the day and works part-time as a delivery rider. I make handmade slime from scratch. I import all the supplies from China , and then I make the finished product. Then I sell it on TikTok shop, Shopify, Ebay and Etsy. I am selling it, and it's like for stress management. I moved to London in November 2024, and after coming here, everyone was like, ' Moving to London is a big step,' and I should not move to London.' Mariam's starting journey, like the other riders, was quite challenging. The first time, I didn't know how the area mattered. So, I picked up my first order from a place close to home. But the delivery address was so far away that there weren't any restaurants there, and I wasn't sure if I will get more orders there. So, I waited there for an hour, hoping order would come . Then, after trying two or three times, I went and parked on Wimbledon High Street. So, the thing is, you get all the orders around High Street. Maybe that's my experience, but all the orders were around High Street. And after delivering those, I got next order really quick. Mariam is not just a delivery rider, but also a mother. I wanted to understand how she manages business, delivery, and all that, while being a mother. I prefer going when my son falls asleep, Or I go when he's in school, these days there is no school, So normally I go when he is at school. I'm not able to go regularly or daily these days because he's home, and as long as he's home, I'm looking after him. So, after he goes to sleep, or sometimes if my sister is off and she's with him, then I do it . Many students in the UK who don't get a job after completing their studies start delivery work after getting a PSW. I completed my studies and I got my PSW. I just got my PSW. PSW is a type of work permit that is granted for two years after completing studies in the UK . I recently completed my studies in May 2025. After that, I've been on a work permit. I came here on student visa and switched to PSW. Mariam isn't alone. There are thousands of riders like her. Who work two to three jobs and work 14 to 16 hours a day. I work 16 to 17 hours here. Shivam, a 28-year-old living in Central London, manages his main job as well as delivery work. I went to meet Shivam to understand how students on PSW manage two to three jobs. I just started in February. I've been working for two months. My main job is at Picture House Cinema. Shivam works 12 to 16 hours a day and also shares food delivery-related content on social media. If you were in your country, you would have choice. Here, we 're here only to earn money, so money or choice is not a question. Money matters only. Do you miss home. Yes, I miss home, but when the money comes, it's not a problem. Now I slowly get used to it. It's been three years here. I asked Shivam an important question: Does his family know he used to deliver there? Yes, everyone knows. There's nothing to hide. All jobs are the same. Working at a restaurant is also a lower-level job. This is also a lower-level job. This is also a similar job. So, are you happy in your life? Yes, I am happy. I am able to make enough money. And I asked the same question to Mariam: Does her family know she is a delivery rider? They had a very weird reaction like, "Okay, this is something very crazy, and I'm getting myself into trouble for no reason." It was kind of weird, like they look down on this job That is how they reacted and what about your parents , Parents are in Pakistan, I mean, do they know you are a delivery rider, no, they don't. And this is Mariam's daily life Mariam isn't alone; I met another female delivery rider, she 's also a mother and works as a delivery rider in Central London. I started five years ago. She used to work full-time as a chef, which she left because she has young children, and this delivery job gives her time flexibility. For me, delivery is better because I can make my time. Because I have kids, I need to help kids in school. I need to drop kids in school. Pick up kids in school. For me is better. But the real question is whether she earns enough money for herself and her kids just by delivering or not? Sometime's not every time, when it is good, then you can make money. When it is not, good, you need to do something different. Delivery is very popular not only among females but also among youngsters due to the flexibility of gig economy. I am not working under anyone. I have a freedom. Don't work under anyone. There is freedom in it A good job is a delivery job. Do it whenever you feel like. If you don't feel like it, call it a day. If we're talking about takeaway and you're in the UK, the number one takeaway in the UK is Chinese. London has the UK's largest Chinatown, with thousands of Chinese restaurants. You'll see thousands of people here, and a large number of food delivery riders waiting and taking deliveries. On most weekends, a single rider picks up over 100 orders and takes them to people's homes. Everywhere you look, there are riders everywhere Look one, two, three, four... Riders everywhere! The busier the area gets, the harder it becomes for riders. And Chinatown is no different. Rates in Chinatown are high because it's always busy and there are traffic issues. Due to traffic, Apps pay extra pounds. It used to be good, but it's been a bit slow for the past year and a half. What is happening now is that we are facing a lot of problems. Earlier it was good. Uber used to give delivery for 6 pounds, 7 pounds something per mile, now it is giving 2 pounds, 3 pounds something for only one mile, so we are facing a lot of problems, in all of this if you depend on delivery then it becomes very difficult to run the house, for now This is how life is in UK I have been doing delivery for the last four years, so this is the fourth year, today is the worst day out of these four years and here Marriam is waiting for her first order. Normally, how long does it take to get a ride? If it's not busy, you won't get any for even half an hour, and if it's moderate, it takes 3-4 minutes So, what are you thinking while waiting ? What's going on in your mind? Normally, I edit my reels. Doesn’t all that waiting get frustrating? Sometimes, yeah… especially when It was too hot Like one day… it was unbearably hot. or it's raining, or the weather isn't favorable, then you get frustrated more quickly. We are ready now! now, and now the wait begins, "Sorry." I say it like that, I talk to myself... Sometimes, they have to wait for hours for an order. Sometimes it comes within two minute… sometimes it can take half an hour. I've been waiting for two hours now and one order so far just sitting and waiting If it's quiet, you have to wait for an hour at least. And a big reason for this is that people do part-time delivery jobs along with their main jobs. This results in more riders and fewer orders. Work is limited here. We get very little work on Thursdays and Thursdays. Some days are good… and some days are quiet. The old riders get plenty of orders… new riders hardly get any. And here, after waiting for half an hour, Marriam got her first order. I think it's 5 to 7 minutes ride. And for that, you'll get £2.90. Wearing gloves feels like your hands aren't working. Meaning, even doing small things are difficult. Is it because of the cold… that you wear gloves? But even then, my hands are a little cold. Like, I don't layer up, as my friend suggested, because you have to wear multiple layers to protect yourself from the cold. But right now, the weather is normal. It's not that bad bad weather is a nightmare for delivery riders . it rains a lot here in London. When it rains… things get a bit difficult. Let's go. So we have delivered this order. After this, another order has come. Another order has come. So how much did you earn from this order as well? I have earned 3 pounds from this order right now. Let me show you some. This is what I earned from this order. 3 pounds. Yes. Okay. Okay. So do you think this ride is worth it at the rate of 3 pounds. Yes, okay. It is 1 km. It takes 5-7 minutes to come. Now, if the time is not busy, then we have to place these orders also. If it is busy, then there is a boost at that time. Boost means you get one and a half times the money . Well, if this same order were boosted 1.5 times, I'd easily earn around 4-5p . When it comes to food delivery income, riders in different areas have different opinions. The pay rate is 12.21, so earning a decent wage is reasonable. I started at 12:00 and currently make 80 pounds. If I work 10 to 12 hours, I'd make 80-90 pounds . Sometimes I could even make 100 pounds. Or I don't think you'll earn as much as your efforts. But those using multiple apps have some benefits. But I only have one app right now. So I don't think it's enough. Then Mariam picked up another order, and we set out to deliver. The stress of food delivery isn't just about getting the order, picking it up, or picking it up. The connectivity is down. Even getting there often presents difficulties. Sometimes the address isn't found, sometimes the app doesn't work, and sometimes some other issue occurs, and that's what happened with Mariam. "Okay, what's the house?" "Sorry, my app is hanging up." So the map is showing me right for "I 'm on, like, outside your house," "House number 2138." And after repeated calls and messages, when the address is found, the customer is already annoyed. The riders' only request to the customer is: "Be patient, customers don't care about them because the customers think you make them already know you." The customer is everything. South Hall, London, is often called "Little India." Most of the people here are Asian , with Indians being the largest number. Pakistani, Afghan, and other South Asian communities also live here . There's also a Butt Kadhai in South Hall. You'll find South Asian restaurants everywhere . Street food is available, and you'll feel as if you're in your own country or wandering through a street in your own city. Enjoying street food. The reason for coming here was that you 'll see numerous food delivery riders standing outside almost every stall, every restaurant, and every cafe. So I thought I'd come here and talk to them about how busy the food delivery business is here and how different it is from Central London. No, deliveries are more in Central London because the population density is much higher there, and there are deliveries here, but not as many as there. Look, there's a difference in earnings between Central London and here. There are no orders placed. There 's just one order placed, and I'm sitting in front of you, waiting. How long do I have to wait? I don't know, brother. There are a lot of delivery drivers here because job options are limited. Those who don't have any job options get their ID and take a bicycle or bike and go out. They make 150 rupees, and sometimes 150 rupees on weekdays and weekends. Even 140 rupees goes by . So if they don't order, then you can think about earning, right? If no one orders, how will I make it? Most people of Indian and Pakistani origin live here, preferring to cook their own food rather than deliver it. However, if we talk about white people, they don't cook; they mostly order in and prefer spending. Whether it's Central London, Chinatown, or Southwark, each place has its own challenges . But one thing I learned from them is that they never forget to celebrate their small joys . Manu gives £1,000 for £100 delivery . That was a great day for me. I went to Europe the next day. The delivery was for £3, but the guy gave me a £50 tip. So, it was a great day for me, and it was a day of delivery. There's no doubt that being a delivery rider is a challenging job. Dangerously so in their week, whether it's a storm, a hurricane, or a long night. These people don't stop, they just keep going. What does mummy say when we call? Mummy doesn't say anything. Mummy just says that if you could take care of the food and drinks, everything would go on. Next time when your order is late, before getting angry, think for a moment that the person bringing your food on two wheels is not just carrying food but also his dreams. I wish brother that he should order food. Whether we get money or anything else, it means that he should order food online. It saves your time also. We provide you food with everything.

- What Is Komoot & How Do We Use It?: Kind: captions Language: en - You might have noticed from some of our recent videos of rides, both near and far, that we often talk something called Komoot, which is, what, exactly? Well, this and this. A route planning and navigation app. Now, I use it all the time. Originally, that came about because it's free, and then, also, because it plays really nicely with the Wahoo head units that we use. And now, two years after I first heard about it, Komoot actually also sponsored GCN, which is super cool. Anyway, I'm gonna talk you through how you use it. There are three layers of functionality to Komoot. Firstly, planning. Secondly, during ride recording and navigation. Either using the smartphone app or synced up to your head unit. And thirdly, post-ride. So, uploading photos, sharing, that kind of thing. Now, I'm not on a bike, as you can see. I also haven't been for a ride yet today, so we're gonna start with the planning phase. You can use the smartphone app for that, both pre-ride and indeed, during ride as well, if you wanna change course, but for in-depth stuff, I'd use the desktop version. There's a bit more space and a bit of added functionality, as well. The first step, will be, of course, to set up an account. As I said, it is free, although you can make purchases in app of maps, easy for you to say, that you can download and use offline. So, if you don't have any phone signal, you've got the data already on your phone. The first screen that you see, is a log of your rides or other people's rides that you've chosen to follow. But we will go swiftly on to the route planning page. Now, you start by entering a start point and a finish point, either by clicking on the map or entering an address. And then you can choose to either have a one-way route or a round trip. Now, if you know where you're going, that's fine, but if you don't, and you're looking for some inspiration, you'll notice there are loads of little red bike icons dotted around the map. Now, these are actually highlights other people have left to help you with your route planning. It could be anything from a beautiful view, to a fantastic little cafe. And if you click on them, you can very simply and quickly add it to your route. Now, the route that I'm planning is one I'm hoping to do this summer with some mates. It's starting from here on the south coast of the UK and going back home. Now, I will enter the start point, Weymouth, finish point, Bristol! And then, I can click to add in a few highlights along the way. Cider, I'll take that one, and then maybe a great road I already know about. To do that, I can just simply click on the map and add it as a Waypoint. We get a number of different routes generated by Komoot's algorithms, and that depends on what type of ride you choose. So, it could be a super fast road ride, it could be a scenic back road touring route, it could be gravel, it could be mountain biking, it could even, actually not riding at all, but hiking, or running. Although, I'm not entirely sure why you'd wanna do that. Anyway, for this particular one, I'm gonna go for a trendy gravel ride by clicking on this icon. As you can see here. So, this is our gravel optimised route, based on what Komoot thinks is the best choice of routes for me to ride from here, to beer. I mean, Bristol. We got all the stats for that route. The ride length, the amount of climbing, the amount of descending, even an estimation of how long it'll take, just as long as you don't have to stop all that much to take photos, like James, or perhaps lie down. And one particularly cool feature is the surfaces and Waytypes summaries function. So, because Komoot uses open cycle maps, or open street maps, then it's got detailed community contributed information about just about every metre of the route. Whether that's a road, or trail, it will know exactly what type of surface you can expect. And so, in this instance, if we change it from a gravel ride to a mountain bike ride, we can suddenly see that the amount of off-road has jumped, we're on Singletrack for 27.3 kilometres, and in total, about 35% of the this route is now unpaved. You can also, then, see, on the interactive elevation profile at the bottom, exactly what surface you can expect to see on either a climb, or a descent. For the control freaks among us, you can, of course, get a bit more control over the route, by adding in more Waypoints to force it in the direction that you want to go. And to get more information to help your decision making, you can toggle through different types of maps. So, from the Komoot map, to open street maps, open cycle maps, where you get information on cycling infrastructure and mountain bike trails as well. Now, the usual caveats do apply with deviating from Komoot's actual specified route. Their algorithms are based on data, so what it knows about the road surface type, and the elevation, things like that. So, if you do choose to go away from it, then, well, that's up to you. You can, of course, choose to ignore Komoot's algorithms altogether, and, in fact, ignore all trails and roads at that with the off grid planning function. Probably more useful for hikers and runners, I would have thought, than cyclists. But that said, I did use it, actually out in Morocco last year when we were able to see from the Google satellite overlay, that there was, indeed, some kind of path over the Atlas Mountains, it just hadn't been mapped yet. Also worth mentioning, as a final point on the planning phase, you can, of course, upload a GPX, a TCX, or a FIT file very easily. And you can either save it as a completed activity, or import it into the route planner where you can then tweak it and modify it as you fancy. Now you're out, there are a few ways in which Komoot can help you navigate on the fly, either by linking up to your head unit, or by using the app directly on your smartphone. Now, we touched earlier on buying maps and then downloading them to your phone, this is perfect if you're going abroad, and you don't want to pay for additional data. It's perfect if you want to save battery, because you can actually navigate whilst your phone is in aeroplane mode. Or, of course, it's essential if you're gonna be going off the beaten track, beyond phone signal. You could make do with the free version, but then you have to rely on your own ability to actually get yourself out of trouble if you get lost. Whereas, if you download the appropriate maps before you go, should you stray off course, it can then help to get you back on track. If you're gonna use your phone only to start navigation, to start recording, you simply hit the giant Start Navigation button and away you go. It will give you turn by turn instructions, both audio, so you can stick your phone in your pocket, and visual, so you can also strap your phone to your handlebars if you so wish. Now, I tend to use mine synced up to my head unit. So, Komoot has advanced integrations with most of the major GPS head units out there. In the case of this Wahoo one, I've actually synced my Komoot account in the Wahoo app, and then all my rides automatically download to the head unit when it's connected to WiFi. If, when you're out riding, you wanna change your route for any reason, if you got a mobile phone signal, doing so is super easy. You simply click wherever you wanna go, additionally, on the map, as if you were planning it in the first place and hey, presto, it will reroute it for you. (exhales sharply) Phew, what an epic. Now, once you have completed your ride, it will automatically save as a private completed tour. Now, that's whether you've been using your phone to record it on or, indeed, in this case the Wahoo, that's automatically synced up to it. Then, you can click into that activity and you can give it a title, you can also upload photos, share it with your mates, tag in whoever joined you along for the ride. Now, it's super easy to upload photos. If you geotagged them at the point that you took them, they will automatically upload to exactly the right place on the map. If you didn't, then you can just drop a pin, and upload it from there. You can also, of course, upload your own highlights, which we touched on earlier on. So, that could be a place, or it could even be a segment of trail or road. You click on the point on the map, and then you upload your own photo, give it title, give it a description. If someone's got there first, then you can either up vote or down vote their highlight and add in your own tip or description and your own photographs as well. So, there we go! Hopefully, you have found this little snapshot about how to use Komoot rather useful. I definitely encourage you to give it a go. If nothing else, you could head over to the GCN profile that we've got on there, because we've actually got loads and loads of routes that we've been doing over the past few weeks and months. Including things like North Coast 500, and our English Epic Ride. - PA FMR Shared Ride Transportation Travel Training: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] before a rider can take a shared ride trip they must register with their local shared ride transportation provider riders can register online or by calling their local transportation provider once registered a rider can schedule a shared ride trip most transportation providers require trips to be booked a minimum of 24 hours in advance when scheduling your trip keep in mind that a shared ride trip will usually take longer than the same trip in a personal vehicle so riders should carry the supplies required to last the duration of their trip including oxygen and other medical aids passengers should be ready and watch for their vehicle within the provided pickup window drivers can only wait five minutes at the pickup location in consideration of other passengers many transit agencies have an automated call system that provides optional reminders the night prior or directly before your trip so check with your local transit agency for details regarding setting up this service drivers must always keep their vehicles in view so they are not permitted to enter homes to assist passengers safety is our number one priority vehicles are fully accessible and can accommodate riders with wheelchairs scooters walkers or other mobility assistance devices if you use a wheelchair or scooter one the driver will exit the vehicle to activate the lift 2. you may back onto the lift or board in the forward position 3. once on the left lock your wheelchair wheels 4. once lifted board the vehicle so you are facing forward five your driver will secure your wheelchair to the floor when boarding the vehicle using the steps you should always have three points of contact one hand and two feet or two hands and one foot it also helps to remember the abc rule a always watch your step b be careful look ahead c clasp the handrail most importantly we recommend you bring any medication or medical supplies that you may need for the entire trip be sure to plan for unexpected delays beyond those needs passengers should be able to carry and control their own items all items must be kept out of the aisles and cannot be placed in seated areas if needed the driver may assist you in securing loose items all riders need to be registered and included in your scheduled trip to ensure there is enough room for everyone on the vehicle you may bring a companion or personal care assistant with you just be aware fees for companions may apply personal care assistants usually ride for free after registration requirements are met passengers must provide appropriate safety seats for children riding with them all service animals as defined by the americans with disabilities act are permitted on all shared ride vehicles we know the unexpected happens riders should contact their local transit agency directly if they will not be ready at their scheduled pickup time or as early as possible when canceling a trip to avoid a no-show on their account no-show policies may vary by local transit agencies to apply for free or reduced fare programs visit findmyride.penndot.pa.gov or contact your local transit agency to see if you are eligible find my ride was developed with funds from the pennsylvania department of transportation and the federal transit administration for more information visit

- Alone Across Gola (2025) | Award-Winning Adventure Documentary: Kind: captions Language: en In 2012, I set off on a 13 month adventure to cycle and wild camp my way across two continents. I'm Jude Kriwald from London, England. I am 20 years old and I’m cycling from England to India. Not going to make it before sunset, I don’t think. At night, I'd shiver in my sleeping bag as temperatures in my tent dropped to minus 31. Just one month later, I’d sweat waterfalls on the tarmac as temperatures soared to 48 degrees. I was chased by packs of wild dogs, held up at gunpoint, hospitalised with typhoid, stayed in countless strangers' homes, tried foods I'd never heard of, and found a sense of adventure which only the open road can bring. I wanted to try living on a bicycle. I could meet lots of interesting people, see beautiful, remote parts of the world. Jump! Jump! It was the best thing I ever did. Fast forward to last year. It's been over ten years since I completed that epic voyage. I'm 31 years old. I had so many hopes of being an adventurer after that trip, but never pursued them further. I'd lie awake at night asking myself why I wasn't out there adventuring. The truth is, pursuing my dream was just too daunting. I was surrounded by typical career escalators, and their thousands of on-ramps made it impossible not to be sucked in. I was stuck, obsessed by dreams I knew I could achieve, but missing the belief and direction to make it happen. There is a cost to doing or not doing anything in life, and it was my mental health that paid the price. When I returned from India, my friends knew me as the happy-go-lucky guy. But that mindset soon faded, replaced by grey mornings, and self-esteem which dropped year after unfulfilling year. I was sure there was no way my partner Nicola would put up with me disappearing to far-flung countries for months at a time. But in the end it was Nicola who provided the spark I needed. ‘Jude,’ she said, ‘For God's sake, go.’ ‘You’ve been talking about this as long as I've known you.’ ‘I'll still be here when you get back.’ ‘Just don't you dare die out there.’ Three months later, I found myself in Liberia, West Africa. I was two months into my new adventure, a three-month cycle alone from Senegal to Liberia. I chose West Africa because I knew so little about it and was desperate to rediscover that feeling of wonder that comes from exploring a new land. Whilst I enjoyed elements of being on the road once more, something was missing. The true adventure that I'd longed for. The type that I used to dream about on those sleepless nights. It eluded me. On top of this, I really struggled to connect with the locals in many villages I visited. My name is Jude. - What? I'd come to West Africa to meet people who lived vastly different lives to me. [White man! White man!] But it was perhaps those same differences that made many interactions so hard. Having an audience watch you sort of make your bed before you’ve left the bedroom. It's quite something. Hello. - Hello. With just three weeks left on my trip, I decided to take matters into my own hands. I think the honest truth is that I'm not loving it so far. And I think that's probably why I made up this crazy challenge. If you look at satellite images of West Africa, you'll see it covered in green. But zoom in further on this northwestern corner of Liberia, where I found myself at that moment, and you'll see a deeper, thicker, luscious green patch, hundreds of miles across. This is the Gola Rainforest. It straddles the border between Liberia and Sierra Leone and is home to the Kissi, Mende, and Gola tribes. I could see a dirt track that weaved right through the centre of the rainforest, except for a short section where the track disappeared. Why was there no track there? Desperate for a challenge to shake up my mindset, I found myself irresistibly drawn to the wilderness. I decided that I would take the dirt track as far as I could into the heart of the rainforest and attempt to bridge that gap in the road. How thick will the jungle be once the track runs out? Is my route even possible? The uncertainty thrilled me and terrified me in equal measures. I’ve stocked up. I've got six baguettes, fifty biscuits, four or five dinners' worth of pasta and noodles. If I can get through to Fayama, it will mean being at the start of the very northern point of a really long track down to the coast. No tarmac, no electricity, no phone signal, and no guarantee I'd make it through to the other side. As I approached the final village, I asked if there was a path which might lead me further into the jungle. It's possible? - Are you walking by foot? - With the bicycle. - No. Unsurprisingly, the track was a dead end. My bike weighed 53 kilos, so with no clear path to cycle on, I'd never make it through. A moment later, the village chief suggested I ask some local men to guide me through the jungle. I was uncomfortable with this at first, but I soon realised it was my only option. And if I carried as much of my own luggage as I could and paid them fairly, maybe it would work out well for everyone. Fayama was a tiny settlement in the rainforest where, according to my maps, the track south continued. If I could just reach Fayama, I'd have cleared the trackless part of the jungle, and be able to complete my three month journey. I soon met Kafo and his friends, the young men who would accompany me through the rainforest. The jungle was like no place I'd seen before. The air was clear, yet I could still see only 20 metres in any direction. Beads of sweat ran down my face and dripped off my chin. We made a good pace, scrambling over jungle rivers, lifting my bike over giant fallen trees, and hacking our way through thick overgrowth. Hold up! About an hour into the trek, I noticed that Kafo kept running ahead with my bike and one of my bags. You don't have to go very far in the jungle to quickly be out of sight. He disappeared with my bike and handlebar bag with all my cash in it. I have zero reason to suspect him. Quite the opposite. But it's a caution in my head I never switch off. The GoPro on my bike, left on by mistake, captured exactly what I'd feared. I haven't realised it yet, but I'm about to be forced into a split-second decision. Do I confront the armed man, who has stolen money from me in the middle of the rainforest, And who I absolutely need to rely on to get out of here? Or do I pretend I didn't see it? Keep the peace. Lose the money. But potentially encourage him to do something even worse. Did you open it? Did you open this? No? I lost some money. I realised that I had to de-escalate. I needed Kafo to think that I didn't think he just robbed me. Otherwise, he'd likely get defensive and I'd be in big trouble. Never before, not on this trip, nor on any other, had I ever felt so vulnerable. My life was truly in the hands of these two strangers, one of whom was carrying a machete and had just tried to rob me. Ah, yeah. This is my money. That’s mine. You took it from here. It’s okay, just give it back. When we get to the end, I’ll pay you. Not before. No problem. I’m very aware of my vulnerability here. I have no idea why I am. No GPS signal with this canopy. These guys know where they are. They're very friendly, but he does have a machete. I couldn't be mad at Kafo. Who am I to say I wouldn't have done the same thing in his situation? The scarcity and hardship of rural Liberians had met the unattainable prosperity of the West. Perhaps what happened is just the unavoidable friction of two worlds colliding. We got through the jungle in one piece and everybody left with more money than they might have, so I had nothing to complain about. I certainly couldn't convince myself that I was less fortunate than Kafo. Deep inside the jungle, we had found a mini oasis, a clearing no bigger than half a football field. Reaching Fayama Town! As I looked around this tiny settlement of six or seven mud huts, I realised I was safe. I let out the biggest sigh of relief of my life. I was unsure if I should pay the man who had just stolen from me. But ultimately, it felt like the right thing to do. Just made it to Fayama after 3 hours in the jungle. I'm with the chief and his son. And another little one. Yeah, quite an experience. Real jungle. You know, there’s sometimes a track, sometimes disappears and the guys get the machete out. Ants latching on. Absolutely piercing pain. And they just grab onto your toe or your ankle and just... I don't know what it is they sink into you, but they sink it. This stuff. It's like Velcro. So painful. Really glad that was over. And these guys have been advising me which track is better to go south from here. But I soon learned the way forward would not be as simple as I'd hoped. This is the road to Jarwajeih? - Yes. - I go straight? - Straight. - Thank you, my friend. I’ll say goodbye. Cheers. The settlement was truly isolated. Fayama was meant to be the beginning of the road south to the next village. Jarwajeih. But this section had been abandoned since the war. You can still trace its vague path, but it's no road. How could my maps be 20 years out of date? There wasn't enough food in this village to sustain another person, and going back the way I came was out of the question. Heading deeper into the jungle to try to find Jarwajeih was my only option. Is this even the same way? I stopped, drank some water. Got out of my wet clothes, wrung out my shirt. Put the tent up. Being very careful to check that there's no ants or anything on all my bags when I put them in. Basically not putting anything on the ground that’s going in my tent. Yeah. Bedding in for a night in the jungle. I've been looking forward to the jungle for ages. I think it hit me a bit quickly. And I took too long... Well, whatever. I took a while to remember all the stuff I prepared. Like certain clothes and so on. But it feels really good to be safe in here now. And I just about managed to get a satellite message out to Nicola Just to say I’m okay, which is really nice. That's a real luxury. I slept pretty well. I had a dream that a man trampled me in my tent. In the end I think it was actually a dream about needing the loo, which I was hesitant to do because just opening the tent means things can get in. Two whole days passed. In that time I managed just eight kilometres each day. There was no road. There was no path. At best, there was a vague semblance of where a dirt track had once been, signalled only by a lack of old trees along an opaque line through the jungle. This is way tougher than I thought it would be. This old path really is no longer used. The jungle had swallowed it whole and now I was vanishing too. I hadn't seen another person since saying goodbye to the chief. Hello? Cycling was impossible. Even pushing my bike was a gruelling feat. Foliage so thick and tall, that I often wondered if I was still on the route of the old road or just lost, heading into the jungle abyss. Where the foliage would clear enough to roll my bike alongside me, fallen trees, four foot thick, lay in my path. With my bike and equipment weighing almost as much as me, the act of lifting over two dozen such trees each day took its toll on my body. It's about a ten foot drop into - oh God, tree trunks with very sharp bits sticking out. No use complaining and no one to complain to. I was in full flow, fully aware of the problems that occurred, but never with a negative emotion attached. Broken equipment, cut limbs, lack of water, ravines with collapsed bridges. What am I doing? And still a huge uncertainty as to whether I'd make it through to a real road on the other side. It’s just taken me about 45-50 minutes to go about 200 metres. Each problem, a challenge to solve. But this is what I came here for. To test my mettle, feel alive, and to feel strong, to put myself back into nature. No safety net but my own cunning to keep me alive. This is my idea of freedom. Three days after leaving Kafo and his friend behind and the sun was setting on another 12 hour day. It’s less than two and a half kilometres to the village, which is just so tempting, but I know that could take hours. Just gotta find a way to be content being in the jungle again, another night. Under the canopy, the flora and fauna were blanketed in a thick darkness. But the forest was anything but still. If my estimations were correct, I'd reach Jarwajeih, and hopefully a real track, by noon tomorrow. The next morning, I finally reached Jarwajeih, where word of my arrival had spread. Welcome! Welcome! Welcome! - Thank you, sir. - Good morning. How are you? - You are a strong man! - Nice to see you. - We were expecting you yesterday. - I was too far. I was camping - [White man! White man!] - Two or three kilometres After spending three days alone in the jungle, I'm overwhelmed by the warm welcome of these strangers and suddenly aware of the deeper connection I’ve been missing on this trip. My name’s Jim. - Jim. Nice to meet you, sir. Goodbye. - Goodbye! - Bye bye. Bye bye. I still had a five day journey ahead of me just to reach a track used by cars, and a further two days to reach the hills by the coast, my finish line. The rough terrain and intense heat were unbearable. Squeezing every last drop of motivation from my body and mind became an increasingly impossible challenge. Today's been the hardest bit. The downhills are almost as slow as the uphills, to be honest. Oh, come on! And I'm trying to keep my morale up because this is pretty tough now. I want to get to civilisation. I've got bread for lunch and I've got noodles for dinner, and then I'm out. I don’t have anything else. Every time I thought the hardest part was over, the jungle would push me to limits I didn’t know I had. Not sure I can cross there. Going to make a dash for it. I’ve got absolutely nothing in my legs. Can’t really work out how much of it is how weak I am, tired, versus how hard this road is. A huge part of me wants to give up, to sit down and stick my thumb out, wait for a ride to get me out of here. But I know that I can't. To give up would break me. The pressure’s too huge and the stakes too high. The weight of the last ten years bears down on me as I simply cannot go back to those dark days. This track is my way out, and suffering, the way through. But despite these challenges, I start to approach the final uphill push and at last, a downhill stretch of cyclable road towards the ocean. Three days later, I arrive at the coast. It’s been 13 days since I last showered. The salt water rushes into my cuts. The pain is beautiful. A visceral trophy of my efforts. Waves wash over me. I float weightlessly and stare up at the empty sky. I later found out that I had lost 15% of my body weight, left behind in the rainforest, in clouds of exhausted breath and puddles of persistent sweat. But I’d gained something, too. Although it took me until that moment to realise it. And what I'd gained is what this story is really about. I realised that courage isn't traipsing my way through rainforests or over mountain passes. I'm in my element in those environments. It's my calling. Is it hard? Yes. But did I really need to be courageous? I don't think so. Courage isn't simply doing the hard thing because you have no other choice. Courage is doing the right thing, specifically when you don't have to. Courage, then, for me, started months before I landed in West Africa. Courage for me was finally admitting to myself that deep, deep down, I want to be an adventurer. I want to spend my life adventuring and helping others to do the same. But most importantly, courage was admitting that my mental health was in my own hands and that no one else is going to improve it for me. It required me to admit that my dreams are possible, as it is all too easy to hide behind the excuse that my dreams aren't possible, and therefore I needn't try. Or that I should wait for someone else to come and improve my mood, and recharge that spring in my step. For me, I get a really big satisfaction out of pedalling and then seeing the ground move beneath me and thinking, ‘Okay, today will move myself 100 kilometres’ ‘and there's a new country around the corner.’ Finally, courage is opening up to the people that I care about and telling them that I want to be an adventurer. Why is that so hard? Because once I admit my dream to people, they can see if I succeed or fail. I can no longer hide behind the socially accepted and easily attained career goals and milestones of life. That earn me a pat on the back, but leave me feeling empty inside. Instead, no matter how daunting, I now have to go and live that dream of mine.

- Duathlon Training & Preparation | How To Plan Your First Duathlon: Kind: captions Language: en-GB Mark: Now, we talk a lot here on the channel about triathlon, but what about their sibling the duathlon. The format of run-bike-run can be quite appealing to cyclists and runners. Maybe just wanting to try their hand at something multi-sport, even to swimmers maybe just wanting to do something completely different. To triathletes, perhaps just wanting a little bit of focus over the off season or the winter. Whatever your reason is today, I'm here to help you with some advice on how to prepare for a duathlon. [music] "Why do duathlon," I hear you say? It's not race if it doesn't start with a swim or contain three different disciplines, et cetera, et cetera. Well, even if your main focus is triathlons, duathlon can be a great and a fun way of mixing your training up, particularly over the off season, when perhaps there aren't so many triathlons going on because perhaps it's too cold to do the open water part of the triathlon, in which case duathlon servers are a very good and quite nice way to get that competitive outlet, gives you a bit of focus and also allows you to practice some of your tactics and strategies ahead of the triathlon season. Then, also, if you're beginner maybe wanting to progress to triathlons then duathlons can be a really nice introduction. Obviously, two disciplines is a lot less scary than three disciplines and, therefore, conserves a very nice introduction to this world of multi-sport. Then, there's nothing wrong If you just don't like swimming. Duathlon does not contain swimming. Therefore, if you just want to do duathlons and that's your end goal, that's absolutely fine. You may be tempted to think that duathlon is completely different to triathlon, and therefore, it requires a very different training regime. Well, in reality, it's really not the case. You can actually perform very well off a normal ordinary triathlon training program. That being said, there may be a few things or tweaks you might want to add and change to your training, just to make sure that you don't get caught out. The first of which being preparing to run before the bike. I know that might sound odd, but as triathletes, we quite often practice running off the bike in what we call "brick sessions," but there's something quite unique about doing a hard run and then a bike with tired legs, and then expecting our legs to work again on that final run. We're expecting quite a lot of our legs. My suggestion to you would be set your bike up on an indoor trainer, finding a little loop outside that you can run on. Starting off with that run route, jumping straight in onto the indoor trainer, and then heading back out onto the run. You can repeat that through multiple times to really get that sensation and feeling. If you're really lucky, you can do it all outside if you've got a nice quiet loop or it's good weather, or all inside if you've got a treadmill. That should really get used to that feeling of doing a hard run into a bike and running off. Most of that is actually dealing with the fatigue in your legs and learning how to run off with that fatigue, all of which will be beneficial and transfer over into triathlon too. Secondly, duathlons are quite often used as a bit of an alternative to triathlons, particularly during the off season, as I mentioned before. If you're really looking to for a while in these duathlons, then you want to be including some race specific training to prepare yourself better for the events. Now in the off season, if we're focusing on triathlons, then we quite often end up doing base training at that time of the year, that can end up feeling and being quite monotonous. Actually, including some of this race specific training in at this time of year, it's not only going to prepare us better for the duathlons, It's also just going to mix things up for us. Finally, you definitely, definitely want to be practicing your transitions, whatever strategy or technique you go for, it's really important that you at least do one session where you just go through it over and over again and go through the motions that you're really well prepared. I saw many good triathletes out there that don't do this and they mess up quite catastrophically. Now, in duathlons, we obviously run first and then run at the end. That may mean that using the same pair of shoes for both the first run and that final run. That needs some practice. We'll go into that a little bit later on. Finally, given that duathlons quite often take place over the wintry months, they can be in less than ideal conditions, quite cold and nippy. You may want to go with some warmer gear for before, maybe even during and after the event, because there is nothing worse than being borderline hypothermic at the start of a duathlon and then not really warming up until about a hundred meters before the finish and then getting cold again. You can wear things like base layers if you're triathlete, I've done that a lot before. You can wear gloves, maybe even a hat. You can take few things actually off If you get too hot during the race, you can drop them off in T1 or T2. Regardless of the result, whether it does help you improve your performance or not, it's going to make it a lot more enjoyable. What pro tips have I got for you? Well, one rather important detail that I've touched on a bit, I haven't really gone into yet, and I really do need to, is your running shoes. A lot of people wear the same set of running shoes for the first run and the second run. That's fine. The idea being that you take off the running shoes in T1, place them down carefully, and, hopefully, they'll be able to slip them on quite quickly in T2. If you really want to kick your duathlon season off with a bang, why not go ready with two sets of running shoes, one set for the first run, and then the second set set up perfectly in T2, so you can slip on nice and quickly. There's no issues with getting a tongue hooked under or anything like that. Then, we've got your socks. Now, hear me out on this one, I know it sounds quite odd, in triathlon we often don't wear socks other than maybe for some long distance events and as we go onto the run. In duathlon, I really would actually recommend you wear socks. It just gives you that extra layer of protection on your feet as you're jumping on and off of the bike. If you're doing flying mounts and dismounts, something that can provide the spectators with quite a lot of amusement, is your socks sticking to the Velcro on your bike shoes or triathlon shoes. I'm talking from experience here. All I do is recommend you go out and just practice doing your transitions with the socks that you're hoping to wear. If you do run into issues, maybe try out some different socks or another method. Then, finally moving on from that, there's the option of actually doing the whole race in your running shoes. I don't see this so often, actually it's quite hard to come by the pedals that you'd need for, they're called Pyro pedals. It's a solid platform. When you come off that first run, you can quickly jump onto your bike, place your feet onto these solid platforms and they hook them in and then jump off the bike and run straight off. I'll be honest this is really for the pointy end of racing for saving those one or two seconds through transition. I hope you have enjoyed today's video. If you have, here are thumbs up button and give this video a like. If you'd like to see more from GTN and follow us on social media, you can do that now. [00:07:09] [END OF AUDIO]
