[shouting instructions] Narrator: These cadets are training to become members of the Royal Canadian Mounted Police. More commonly known as the Mounties. But before they don the iconic scarlet tunic known as the red serge, all cadets have to graduate from the RCMP's 26-week training program, which combines intense tactical training with customs and traditions... Cadet: To the queen. Cadets: To the queen. Narrator: That are steeped in Canadian history. Every year, about 1,000 cadets graduate from the academy before joining the 20,000 RCMP officers serving across the country. On a bitterly cold week in December, we spent five days at the academy, where we saw different troops at various stages of the 26-week training program. On day one, a new troop is welcomed by the academy's commanding officer. Jasmin Breton: Everybody wants you to be successful. We're incredibly happy that you're finally sitting in these seats as members of Troop 29. And we want you to be successful. But we're not gonna do it for you. You have to do it for yourselves. Narrator: The application process, which includes a polygraph test, can take about eight months. Breton: Everybody took a year out of your life to finally get here. We sometimes see cadets go home after 24 hours. It takes probably a couple of weeks to get used to the routine. So give it at least a couple of weeks. Work hard, have some fun, and good luck. Are we a kinder police organization? That is a comment that we actually verbally say out loud now. We're in the service industry. We're out there to serve the Canadian public. So if we create that type of environment, we're hopeful that the cadets will remember that and when they are out in their various communities that they will continue to work with the communities as they were trained. Narrator: Training happens here, at RCMP Academy, Depot Division, located in Regina, Saskatchewan, about 100 miles north of the US border. It's a region of Canada known as the Prairie, where, during our visit to Regina, sustained winds combined with the low temperature made it consistently feel about negative 25 degrees Fahrenheit. The starting salary for a constable in the RCMP is about $53,000 a year. After three years, it increases to about $86,000. Applicants must be at least 19 years old and willing to relocate anywhere in Canada, including remote territories like Yukon and Nunavut. The Royal Canadian Mounted Police as we know it today was founded in 1920. Movie clip: The Canadian Rockies. The legendary Mounties are the symbol of law and order in this vast domain. Narrator: Although they're called Mounties, the RCMP isn't a mounted police force, and horses are only used for ceremonial events. ♪ Here come the Mounties ♪ ♪ To get the man they're after now ♪ Mike McGinley: We're not what you see in the movies from Hollywood. People see the red jackets, pointy hats on black horses, and they think that's what RCMP is. But we're police officers first and foremost, and I think that's probably the biggest misconception. [garbled radio] The reality is policing back roads and small towns by yourself. We are a police force; that's the biggest message. Narrator: And it all starts here, at Depot. Cadet: I need you to stand up. You're under arrest for assault with a weapon. Narrator: Where most of the training is focused on mastering police procedure, practiced in realistic scenarios where cadets play the roles of both the officer and the suspect. Cadet: Anything that you do say to me can be used as evidence. Do you understand? Cadet: Yeah. I just want you to know that I messed up. I didn't mean to. I'm not that type of person. Cadet: OK, good to know. Cadet: Can I get a 10-28, 10-29 PROS on a Saskatchewan marker when ready? Operator: 10-4, go ahead. Breton: We're trying to prepare them for the realities of policing. Cadet: Do you have anything on you that could hurt me or you? Breton: Policing isn't an easy profession. We make the program such that they will be physically and mentally prepared. But they also have to have common sense; they have to know their powers of arrest; they have to know the law. So it's a real combination of two things that need to happen all at once. Narrator: Male and female cadets train together at Depot, and even face each other in Police Defensive Tactics training, or PDT. Audrey Soucy: Fight! Obviously, in policing, we don't choose our opponent. We're not gonna be always matched with a normal size like you would see in the sport of boxing or MMA. Most of our cadets come here without any backgrounds at all. Some of them have never been involved in a fight. So it's very difficult for them to understand how to deal with a situation like that. Narrator: Cadets are faced with additional disadvantages. Sometimes they're only allowed to use one hand, or they're only allowed to use leg strikes. Another exercise requires one combatant to spin around in circles before the fight begins. Soucy: Go! Fight! If you get into a fight with an opponent out there in the street, it could happen that you get hit in the head and that can create, like, some kind of dizziness. Little bit of a concussion type of scenario. We want to make sure that they have this kind of understanding without putting them into a concussion, obviously, 'cause we don't want them to be hurting themself. Narrator: But with punches flying, minor injuries do occur. Soucy: Sometimes some things like that happen. We try to mitigate that as much as we can, so. It's almost a good thing he can feel that so then if it happens in the field, he knows how to deal with it. Cadet: Put your hands up, OK? Cadet: My hands are up. My hands are up! Narrator: In this scenario... Cadet: You're under arrest! Get down on the floor! Narrator: Cadets attempt to subdue an aggressive assailant and apply handcuffs. But it's not easy. To make the scenario more challenging, cadets are matched up with assailants who are bigger and stronger than they are... Cadet: Trying to take his arms down. Narrator: Whose objective is to try and grab the gun and baton from the cadet's duty belt. Rebecca Cicansky: The reason that we do this is basically to allow the police officer to reassess the totality of that situation. And now that jeopardy has raised for the police officer, they should be able to reassess that situation. Cadet: I got his legs. Cicansky: Communication is the big key here. They wanna communicate with their partner... Cadet: Call for backup! Cicansky: And then come up with a new plan based on their risk assessment, which is always evolving. McGinley: Nobody dies at Depot. This is the place to make mistakes. 'Cause when police officers make mistakes, people get hurt. Some of the stuff they see and will see is not nice. And we need to help prepare them for that physically and mentally. Narrator: Being exposed to pepper spray is a common part of law-enforcement training, and it's no different in the RCMP. [cadet shouting] Tylor Goulet: We want them to get an appreciation for using pepper spray so they understand, essentially, that it's painful and so they understand that it works, it's effective. And that they can fight through it if they are exposed to OC spray themself. They do 50 jumping jacks. We just wanna get their heart rate up, just to simulate as if they were in some sort of a physical altercation, perhaps. They then go into the actual chamber where it's been contaminated with pepper spray. You've already gone 10-19, I said just make the arrest. And then they have to make an arrest on the subject inside that chamber, keeping their eyes open, being aware of their surroundings, and fighting through that painful experience. Narrator: But at Depot, cadets take it to another level. After being exposed, cadets exit the facility and perform an arrest in the freezing cold. Cadet: Would you like to speak to a lawyer when we get back to the office? Goulet: They probably barely notice the cold when they're out there, immediately after the exposure. It's only once that the effects have worn off that they realize it's actually cold outside. [cadet coughing] It actually will help somewhat with numbing that pain of the pepper spray once they get back into a warmer environment. Instructor: Just had to adjust the volume. Is everybody able to hear me over there? Lane one, can you hear me OK, you guys can hear me OK? K, 'cause things that I say are so important. Narrator: Cadets fire between 800 and 900 rounds during their 65 hours of training on the firing range. Ryan Peters: Probably 15%, 20% of the cadets that show up have some sort of firearms experience, whether that's pistols, shotgun, rifle, or hunting. We're able to take someone with absolutely no pistol or firearms experience and make them quite good shooters and successful through the program. So, week two, they're actually issued their service pistols, Smith & Wesson 5946. That is their pistol. And it will follow them through their career until they so retire or move on to something else. Firearms is a very important skill set to have, and if the day ever comes that you actually need to rely on your pistol to protect a human life, it's going to be a situation where we hope that you're proficient and you're prepared for that day when it does come. Training video: I was here yesterday! I'm trying to make a living! Cadet: Drop the knife, sir! Video: You're always jacking me up! Instructor: OK, we're gonna pause this scenario. Narrator: Cadets interact with a video in which role-players create a scenario that tests judgment... Cadet: Sir, can I see your hands? Video: I told you already. Narrator: And how do de-escalate a threatening situation. The judgment simulation room is also equipped with what's known as a shoot-back cannon, which fires projectiles at cadets. Curtis Parker: We can basically give them a little shot with the shoot-back cannon, and it will remind them to go use cover. So, it only needs once or twice, and then they quickly get behind cover. Narrator: Every cadet is required to pass the PARE test. PARE stands for Physical Ability Requirement Evaluation. Instructor: We're gonna run hard. Everyone's gonna get superior today. Cadet: Oh, yeah. Narrator: And it's used to determine a cadet's physical ability for duty. Instructor: Ready, go! Instructor: Pace yourself, buddy. The PARE is the current job standard test that we use. It's the measure of success for fitness and physical ability when cadets graduate from Depot. So if a cadet is unsuccessful at PARE, then they will not graduate from our program. The cadets will run six laps of an obstacle course that includes running over a set of stairs twice in each lap as well as jumping over some lower hurdles and going over a 3-foot barrier, laying on the ground, and getting up. So, they go through that circuit six times, and then they move into what we call the push-pull station, that replicates that altercation. And they need to push and maneuver a weight six times. The weight is 70 pounds. The standard for what we call a pass is four minutes and 45 seconds. In policing, the physical demands are unpredictable, and it's difficult to do your job well or safely if you are unfit. Narrator: Cadets are also timed in scenario-specific training like this exercise, where the objective is to load up a truck bed with various life-saving supplies. Each item weighs about 50 pounds, and the goal is to safely load the truck in less than two minutes. Instructor: Time. Good one, 2:02.9. [dog barking] Narrator: Training moves outside when cadets work with a dog handler. Brent Bates: This is Bailey. She was my partner as a police dog. I spent seven years on the road with her as a police dog when we were partners. She goes on what are called uniform runs. It's supposed to simulate running with a dog handler, pursuing a suspect, or looking for a lost person. Instructor: Come on, let's go! Bates: We end up going over a lot of fences when we're chasing bad guys. It's just the nature of how our work is. To try and get a dog over a fence, our main concern is the safety of the dog. We don't want the dog getting hurt chasing somebody. And so there's certain ways to get the dogs over the fence. They utilize their harness, so what we usually do is get one person at the top of the fence, sit on top of the fence, and then I hand the dog up to them. And their job is to make sure she doesn't come back over the fence or jump down the other side and hurt herself. We're lucky with Bailey. Bailey's very friendly and very easy to work around. The cadets use it as a bit of a learning tool, to show them how to be around the dog and what the dog looks like when it's actually working. Jonathan Neufeld: What a tremendous opportunity. A turning point for this troop. Narrator: Cadets have to pass a rigorous inspection of their barracks, known in the RCMP as pits. Cadets lay out all components of their duty belt on their beds and stand by while an officer inspects all of their clothing and personal belongings. Neufeld: Look what you have done. A cow sacrificed their life for your belt. Look what you've done to it. ヤギの足跡を見つけた場 所に戻ってきました。 で、ヤギの鳴き真似をして… ヤギを… おびき寄せてみます。 ゴートマンの霊よ… あなたの森にいる我 々の前に姿を現せ… よし。 この… 未確認生物というか… 伝説というか、 呼び方は何でもいいけど、 知ってる人は… そんなに多くない。 だから、 もし君が俺たちにヤバ い答えとかくれたら、 君のこと、 もっと多くの人が知る ようになるかもよ。 - ちょっとかっこいいだろ。 謎めいてるけど。 ポープリック、お前か？ 英語話す？ おい、違うだろ。 おい… ああ、まあ、 近いっちゃ近いか。 変な韓国語みたいだな。 おい、黙れよ。 違う。 よし、 スピリットボックス を試してみよう。 何か聞こえるか確認しつつ… あれにも注意して。 この森に俺たち以 外の霊はいるか？ 咳しただけだ。 全然聞こえなかった。 「去れ」って聞こえた。 出ていけってことか？ 急げ。 なぜだ？ なぜ？ 何？ はっきりしろ。 待て？ 何を待つんだ？ 行動。 行動？ 行動？… どんな行動？ 一歩ずつ？ おい、なんか音が聞こえて、 そしたら「森からの音 」って言ったんだ。 そう聞こえた。 まさか。 もしここにいるなら、 レムポッドを鳴らせるか？ 棒に触るか、 近くを歩くだけでいい。 無理か？ 今「ハロー」って言ったぞ。 朝ふたり死ぬ。 じゅうにんが死ぬ。 ふたり。 ふたり？ うわ、悲しすぎる。 翼はあったのに、 心がダメだった。 おい、なんか… 車が… かなり古い車があるぞ。 ああ、これはすごい。 ゴートマンの最初 の目撃情報は、 車？ 車を襲ったって話だったな。 マジで？ そこにバンパーがある。 おい、 前に見せたあれやろうぜ、 ほら、怖い話のやつ。 「ふたりの男が… 怖い暗い森に入りました。 ここに化け物がい ないといいな、 とひとりが言いました。 時速50マイルは 出てたはずだ。 しがみついてたんだぞ？ 彼女はまだしがみついてて、 彼はただ友達を見てたんだ。 そう、彼女を。 彼女が死んだんだ。 マジかよ…。 怖すぎる。 電車の速さは本当 に恐ろしかった。 俺たちは今、 この場所の真の恐怖と、 多くの人がそうで あったように、 橋の上に出てしまうと いう罠にどれほど簡単 に陥ってしまうかを身 をもって理解した。 それで、 一番最初は17歳 の少年だった。 マジで… ふたりに度胸試しで渡るよ うそそのかされたんだ。 それは想像できない。 高校生なら絶対やっ てたバカなことだ。 でも、別に… それに、 彼を責めることなん てできないだろ。 ああ、それで、その次だ。 その次は二十数年後で、 その男は落ちたけ ど助かったんだ。 その一年後、 ジョン・ケネス・リストと いう男性が亡くなった。 さらにその一年後… デイビッドという十九歳 の少年が亡くなった。 また一年後… 毎年だよ。 本当にヤバいな。 本当にすごいよな… 一九六八年から今まで、 実に半世紀以上もずっ と続いているんだ。 こんなにも長い間、 この悲劇が続いてるなんて、 正直信じられないよ。 一体いつになったら、 この悲惨な状況に何らかの対 策が講じられるんだろうか。 でも、なんで渡るんだよ、 理由なんてないだろ。 スリルのためだろ。 俺たちがやってること。 絶対消えない。 対策もされない。 だろうな。 幽霊とか怪物とか、 そういうのはある程 度信じてるけど、 ポープリック・モンスタ ーがいるとは思わない。 噂が独り歩きして、 調査に来た人たちが死んで、 それでさらに噂が 大きくなって、 その繰り返しなんだと思う。 トミーが俺よりうま くまとめてくれた。 絶対に、 この橋を渡らな いでください。 ここで亡くなった多くの方々 のご冥福をお祈りします。 彼らの多くは、 この謎を解き明かそうと、 俺と同じ情熱と好奇心 を持っていました。 絶対にこの橋には行 かないでください。

which had openings and tunnels leading around large Boulders there was a large ramped area called the slide where the entire room sloped down at a 45° angle with a rope to guide you down this cave system was well mapped in most of the traversible areas there were also well-known areas that were more challenging like the birth canal or the aorta crawl these areas were tight and difficult to pass had just recently been opened back up to the public after being closed for several years because of cavers getting stuck in these difficult squeezes they were passable for some but others would find canal and consulted the map for its location it was known to be a long tight passage with a particularly tight squeeze near the end if you could just get past the narrowest part there would be a small room at the end to stand up and turn around they knew that the birth canal would be tight and could be Panic inducing even for experienced cavers they spread out to try to find this particular tunnel John and Josh took the passage on the left moving through a small tunnel for around 15 ft it undulated up and down in several elevations over the next 10 ft there were outcroppings of rocks and tight squeezes that required a caver to twist their body in several orientations to get shoulders past then hips and finally twisting legs so that they Bend in the proper direction it was not a matter of Simply pulling your body forcefully through a tight hole this area was called the cork screw because the few who had been through it were small and flex ible using all their skills to maneuver slowly through the panic inducing contortions after the cork screw area the passage angles up for another 5 ft or so then turns down over a rock ledge with a sharp 90° Bend to the left up until now cavers had used this small area after the cork screw to turn back on themselves if needed and head back through the difficult Section 5 years before a 16-year-old boy named Brock Clark was stuck in this cork area for his pressed body to ball up for a turn he pushed forward to the ledge area and squeezed over the top of it it was a tight area to get through but he thought he could see it open up down below - 5 Tips to Improve Your Horse Jumping Technique (With FEI II Show Jumping Coach Krystal Kelly) #horse: Kind: captions Language: en hey there show jumping enthusiasts today we have an exciting video for you if you want to take your horse jumping technique to the next level then you've come to the right place we've got five amazing tips that are sure to improve your performance in no time don't forget to hit that subscribe button and turn on notifications to stay up to date with all of our latest videos number one perfect your positioning the first tip to improve your show jumping technique is all about your position make sure you have a solid base in the saddle with your stirrups on the balls of your feet and your legs wrapped around your horse's belly keep your weight centered over your horse's center of gravity and maintain a tall posture with your back flat this will help you maintain your balance and stay in control throughout the jumps number two practice your timing the second tip is to work on your timing good timing is crucial for a successful jump so practice your approach to the jump and your takeoff as you approach the jump sit back and bring your horse to a balanced controlled Canter keep your eyes up and look ahead to the next obstacle as you take off be sure to follow your horse's motion and stay centered in the saddle number three master your maneuvering the third tip is all about maneuvering you need to be able to guide your horse through the jump course with precision and accuracy practice your turns and Transitions and make sure you can guide your horse through tight spaces and over tricky obstacles the more control you have over your horse and are able to adjust in between the fences the more successful your jumps will be number four build up your Fitness the fourth tip is to build up your horse's Fitness show jumping is a physically demanding sport and your horse needs to be in top condition to perform at its best make sure you're giving your horse the right nutrition and exercise to build up its strength stamina and Agility this will help your horse perform at its best and prevent injury poles and grit exercises as well as a lot of dressage training will really help with this number five invest in quality Equipment the final tip is to invest in quality Equipment your horse's gear such as the saddle Bridle and Boots can have a big impact on its performance invest in high quality equipment that fits your horse properly and is designed specifically for show jumping this can help your horse feel more comfortable and confident during jumps and also help prevent injury additionally consider investing in gear for yourself such as high quality helmet and custom made riding boots that provide proper protection and support quality Equipment may be more expensive but it can make a significant difference in your jumping performance and safety there you have it five amazing tips to improve your horse jumping technique practice these tips regularly and you'll be sure to see a noticeable Improvement in your performance don't forget to hit that subscribe button and turn on your notifications for more great content thanks everyone for watching I'm your coach Crystal Kelly

[Music] Tip number one, stretch and warm up properly. Anytime you're hobby horsing, it's super important to remember to stretch beforehand and warm up, but especially when you're learning a new jumping form, I find it's extra important that you remember to stretch your body out beforehand. Next, I want to teach you my stretch for my jumping form. You're going to bring your front leg forward at a nice jumping degree angle and your back leg backwards at a 90. You're going to really remember to breathe and rise up through the crown of your head. Envision that hip that really feels tense and crunched up dropping into the floor. Really remember to breathe. If you feel comfortable, do some leaning forward to uh imitate your two point. Make sure you lower your jumps to a super nice low height so that you can warm up easily. Then make sure you go through walk, trot, and cander both sides before you start jumping and figuring out your jumping form. Tip number two, practice at a walk. Here you see I approach the jump and just think out how the jumping form is going to work for me. Bringing my legs in the position they're going to be in super slowly and just kind of feeling it out with my body. Making sure I do it both directions and just really feel it out. If that feels pretty good, you can raise the jump and feel it out at a more realistic height. Really, we're taking a lot of time here to feel out how the form feels on our body over a jump. Once you've done that, go ahead and try it at a trot or caner, remembering to just take it easy, do it low, and really feel out how it's going to be for you. Practice double on your hard side. If that's going great, then we can raise it. But before we jump this, we're going to walk it out again. Think out your striding and feel out how you're going to do it over that bigger jump. Then go for it. Yes. Okay, that brings us to tip three. Lower the jump. It's so easy to get distracted and want to get higher and higher. But the best thing to do for your body, especially when learning a new form, is to lower the jump. Anytime you knock it or have trouble, don't go again. Lower the jump and feel it out over the lower jump. Again, I know it's really easy to want to go high, but you're not going to be able to go high if your form is sloppy. If you've done it good over the lower jump, you can raise it again. In the end, taking your time to learn something new, especially for jumping, is going to end up yielding much better results than rushing it. If you're really knocking a lot and it feels super hard, that's going to bring us to tip five, which is walk it out or try again. It's totally okay to take a break occasionally. Try it another day or walk it out for a while and then come back to it. If you try multiple times and it feels really uncomfortable, maybe look to a different jumping form. Just because your favorite jumper uses a form doesn't mean that you have to use it. You might find something that's better for you. If it's going well, though, raise it a bit. Have some fun. Don't raise it too high, though. You don't want to rush things. Just raise it to a height that feels manageable. If it's going good, don't push yourself too hard. End it on a good note. that if you're not a professional rider who's out there all the time jumping you are going to get a variety of distances you just need to learn to accept it because when you see a deep distance and you lift your chin engage your core you encourage the Worcester rock back he jumps a nice fence if it's a long stride you close your leg you be nice and positive but it's when you see a stride that you don't like and you freeze and do nothing that's when the wheels fall off so what this exercise does is it teaches you to trust what you see and to accept it and the more you start accepting us the - HOW TO DEAL WITH HORSE RIDING COMPETITION NERVES | Equestrian Mindset: Kind: captions Language: en Hi guys, welcome back to my channel. Now fingers crossed, it looks like we should all be back out competing by the beginning of April, which is brilliant. However, being that we've all been out of action for so long, I'm pretty sure the competition nerves are probably going to hit us fairly hard. And I'm someone that's always suffered from them. So this is my routine to dealing with the nerves. Breathing properly is one of the simplest ways we can do to help with the nerves. For me, I tend to find my nerves really start creeping in when I'm in the lorry on the way there, or maybe just before I'm about to go in and do a round. That's when I feel my nerves are at their worst. So for me, just taking a couple of really deep, slow breaths takes my focus away from the competition for a moment, and also just gets my heart rate back down. And then I feel like I can kind of refocus and get on with the job. Visualization has been the biggest help to me. It's the one thing that I feel really changed how I cope with my nerves. For me, I'm somebody that the moment I wake up on the day of a show, I feel nervous. So I like to visualize the whole day through. So literally from the moment I get up to getting the horse ready on the lorry, sat in the lorry even. And I do this in kind of fast forward mode. And then when I get to like the show in my head, I will then slow down and really ride the warm-up, making sure that I go through all the movements I want to do to help get that horse ready. And then for this instance, if I'm doing a dressage test, I'll run through the test step-by-step, making sure, you know, I use my corners, that I ride the movement how I want it to be ridden, that I'm even feeling positive as I'm kind of going through it in my head. So as much detail as I can. And then I find that when I get to the show on the actual day, that I've run through this so many times in my head that I'm so much calmer for it. Now obviously, if you're an eventer or a show jumper, you are not always going to know what the course is going to be. In that instance, I tend to just look online, check that I know what the venue looks like, the show jumps they use, and I would make up a course in my head. Still making sure that I hit every stride right, that I've got that perfect canter, that powerful canter to jump the fences out. And that will still help me for on the day. Visualization would be my number one tool to use to help with my mindset. It's a real game-changer. Finding out that anxious and excited emotions are almost identical and the body can't tell the difference between the two, was a really interesting find for me. And actually something that I've put into practice and found really works. It's a little bit crazy, but it does. So for me, it means that anytime I think of a certain competition that's coming up for me, or the week before a show, or even the day of the show, anytime I start feeling anxious and concerned about it for whatever reason, I try and switch it around my head and go, "Actually, this feeling is me super excited." Which deep down is true. I am excited to compete, otherwise I wouldn't do it and the rest of us wouldn't do it. But I try and change that feeling from thinking this is anxious feelings to this is excitement. This is my body going, "Yes, we cannot wait to get out and do what we love the most." Just thinking like that will help. Staying positive can be easier said than done, especially for me. When I was younger, the moment I felt nervous, the negative thoughts sort of swamped my head. But definitely now I'm older, I'm much better. And I try to just remind myself that it is good fun, that we're meant to go out and enjoy ourselves, and to use positive language, even when I'm slightly concerned or feeling a little bit stressed, just still trying to use positive language to just help the day go a little more smoothly. Embracing your nerves is perhaps something that I've learned to do later on in my kind of competition riding. Because at the end of the day, being nervous is part of your body's way of helping you to do your best. And I think if you think like that, then the nerves won't feel quite so bad. Cuz I mean, it the adrenaline is helping me to be sharper, and I feel personally ride better. So I've got to learn to embrace them, rather than think that they're such a negative. And I do think that really does help how you cope with your nerves if you think that actually they're benefiting you. Most importantly, we just have to remember we are so lucky to do such an incredible sport and get to ride these beautiful animals, and to try and embrace those horrible nerves when they affect us. Anyway, I hope this video is of some help, and I do feel sorry for anyone who has really bad nerves, cuz I get them too, and it can put a dampener on the day, but don't let them take over.

  1. @fiorideisole [4 likes]: Thank you for making this, Shelby! I know that owning a horse is still a long ways away from me, unfortunately. I recently had to put off lessons for a little while in order to build up a savings again. I want all of the students (particularly American students) who are reading this to know that horse ownership may seem affordable right now but once you graduate college and have to start paying back your student loans and other life expenses, shit will get expensive FAST. Please fully think through how you will be able to afford a horse for long term. Always put the best interest for the animal in mind FIRST over what you may want for yourself.

  5. @Patti-1962 [3 likes]: Owning a horse is SO expensive. I owned a horse while growing up, but I boarded at a barn where I had to go feed her everyday and clean her stall etc . Just to let you know how old I am, my parents paid $20 per month to board my horse. I felt very fortunate to be a stay at home mom for my two boys, but when they were both in school... I started thinking about owning a horse again. I am a violinist, so I started a business teaching lessons after school. Once I built my business, I bought my mare. I made a great living from my job, but most of my salary went to her expenses.($600-$1000 per month, depending on what she needed from the vet and farrier.) The initial cost of purchasing my horse, saddle, bridle and ALL the other things I needed was around $10,000. This is the reality of horse ownership. I know it can be done cheaper, but I think my experience is more the norm. 
I was the only adult rider at my barn, so I had the mornings alone to ride( the ring to myself!) and take my lessons!  My trainer rented the barn, so she allowed me to take care of the barn on weekends. This is when many of the riders/ horses and our trainer were at shows. I had full responsibility for the barn (cleaning stalls, turn out and putting on rugs, giving medicine, and whatever else needed doing). Then she would give me free lessons! My husband and sons would come with me to help. My sons are now married with children of their own, but the STILL talk about how much fun it was to spend the weekends at the barn when they were younger!!!!

known as an in-kind collaboration or partnership&nbsp;&nbsp; so technically your compensation in that situation&nbsp; is the product that you receive you're not getting&nbsp;&nbsp; paid on top of it now personally I think that&nbsp; creators should always be paid when there are&nbsp;&nbsp; content deliverables that's just my opinion but&nbsp; I also recognize there could be circumstances&nbsp;&nbsp; where an in-kind collaboration makes sense if&nbsp; working with this brand because they weren't&nbsp;&nbsp; compensated fairly from the get-go writing a&nbsp; good pitch or having a a nice looking pitch deck&nbsp;&nbsp; is massively massively massively important um&nbsp; an absolutely enormous novel of an email as your&nbsp;&nbsp; first email to that person I've made that mistake&nbsp; plenty of times and you know what when anybody&nbsp;&nbsp; gets an email that that's that long just out of&nbsp; the blue when you already got a busy day you're&nbsp;&nbsp; probably not going to look at it because just&nbsp; overwhelming so over the last couple of years&nbsp;&nbsp; I have begun sending a much much shorter email&nbsp; just like a maybe a paragraph or two just kind&nbsp;&nbsp; of explaining very very quickly what we're doing&nbsp; just asking them hey can I send a more complete&nbsp;&nbsp; pitch to you cuz if you do that one it primes them&nbsp; for the giant novel that you're about to send and&nbsp;&nbsp; then two if you're sending a massive email with&nbsp; links and attachments and all this kind of stuff&nbsp;&nbsp; 99 times out of 100 it's going to get sent right&nbsp; to the junk folder because of those links because&nbsp;&nbsp; of the attachments and all that and so you kind&nbsp; of need to get a conversation going with with&nbsp;&nbsp; that person so that when you send the big email&nbsp; afterwards it's going to go to their inbox and not&nbsp;&nbsp; straight into the spam folder another example is&nbsp; all of that Sim racing gear that you saw earlier&nbsp;&nbsp; that is a lot of very rad stuff for Sim racing we&nbsp; are entering once again into a category or sport&nbsp;&nbsp; that we have never been in before we have nothing&nbsp; to show Automotive wise except for one video that&nbsp;&nbsp; I made last year about our Land Rover which is a&nbsp; - How The Top 1% Deal With Performance Anxiety: Kind: captions Language: en on August 4th 2024 fans around the world witnessed arguably the greatest ever 100 meters Final in Olympic history during the entrances of the athletes the race favorite Noah LS burst through the doors not with a walk but with a roaring set of energetic bounces these carried him a good 50 MERS up the track away from his opponents who had all rather more calmly taken to their respective lanes for ly this was the first step to challing his pre-race nerves in a way that filled him with the confidence to execute however free flowing race so in the last seconds prior to the race he brought himself way back down the cameras caught him repeatedly uttering the words to himself thank you God this was of course spiritual but it was just as much psychological at that moment LS was practicing what's known as grounding selft talk a technique that allowed him to channel his nerves into focused energy and a couple of minutes later LS crossed the line first to win Olympic gold by just 5 thousands of a second so what on Earth was L playing at why did he go from explosive WWE style entrance to a calm grounded competitor in the space of a couple of minutes well it's all part of a wider strategy one where LS has got to a point where he's able to leverage and use his pre-race nerves to his Advantage he can strategically Channel nervous and anxious energy into a fuel that has made him become the fastest man in the world and the good news is the same strategies can be used by all athletes whether you're also a track athlete gearing up for a race a fighter heading to the ring for a bout or playing any kind of game or match you can use your nerves to help your performance rather than hinder it and this video will tell you exactly how right so what exactly was happening in those final moments before Noah LS won Olympic gold to understand let's talk about the jks Dodson law the principle explains the relationship between arousal levels in other words how pumped up or calm you are and your performance and the idea is simple too little arousal and you're understimulated too much and you're overwhelmed but there's a sweet spot in the Middle where performance Peaks that's where the magic happens for athletes finding this optimal arousal state is crucial get it wrong and your nerves might just sabotage your performance get it right and those same nerves can become your greatest asset so the first thing athletes must realize is that you actually do want to feel some of the physical characteristics of being nervous before performances because if you don't that's where you're understimulated and if that happens your body won't wake up quick enough to run a fast race or you won't be as reactive as your opponents and they'll sweep you aside but you don't want these feelings to become too intense you don't want your heart rate to increase so much that it feels like it's bursting out of your chest or you don't want the butterflies in your stomach to feel so intense that you want to puke because in those moments where you're over aroused you're more prone to making mistakes errors or have poor form and execution because you're too tense or just too agitated instead and this is the difficult part you want to find the sweet spot where you're hyped up but not too hyped up but at the same time you're composed but not too calm to the point where you're not switched on at all so how as an athlete can you use this law to your advantage well the key is managing your arousal levels through what I call uppers and downers uppers are techniques that boost your energy and get you fired up think of things like upbeat music high energy warm-ups or even a powerful pep talk these are great for sports where you need explosive power like sprinting or weightlifting on the flip side downers are all about calming your nerves bringing you back down to a state of focus relaxation techniques like deep breathing grounding selft talk or even visualizing a peaceful scene in your mind this is especially useful in sports that require precision and control like archery or pistol shooting now here's where it gets tricky finding this balance isn't something that happens overnight it takes years of practice self-awareness and experimentation athletes like noos have spent countless hours fine-tuning their approach to pre-performance arousal they've learned how to read their bodies and Minds perfectly understanding when they need to ramp up the energy and when they need to dial it back so if we look at LS before the 100 meters Olympic final You could argue that he uses his explosive entrance to really hype himself up and even deliberately take himself into the higher arousal Zone and then he engages in his grounding self talk to just nudge himself back into the optimal arousal Zone just before getting into the starting blocks and sometimes certain uppers like no's hyped up entrance actually Burns off some of that excess nervous energy so that you're then forcing your mind and body to then nudge back into the greater sense of calmness and composure that's required for Peak Performance the ability to shift between these Stakes with Precision is what sets Elite athletes apart it's not just about knowing the techniques it's about mastering the timing and intensity of each one and that's something that takes a lot of time patience and a lot of trial and error to get right but once you do it can make all the difference between a good performance and a gold medal winning one so how can you start applying these techniques to your own game well it's impossible for me to say because honestly it varies so much sport to Sport and also what type of competitor and athlete you are but here are three simple steps so that you can work this out for yourself one identify your optimal arousal level take the time to actually understand how your body reacts under different levels of arousal reflect on past performances to identify when you felt just right meaning you're not too hyped up but also not too calm this self-awareness is the foundation for mastering your optimal pre-performance state to experiment with uppers and downers incorporate both uppers and downers into your pregame routine test out different combinations to see what works best for you for example try some deep breathing or meditation a couple of hours before your event starting if you're feeling overly nervous and then use some Hype music to bring you up in the last 30 minutes or so before performing and if you're struggling for ideas here I've put together a simple list of 30 effective uppers and downers you can check that out using the link below it's completely free and then lastly refine through practice remember this is a process that takes time continuously refine your approach by practicing these techniques during training and less critical competitions - How stress affects your brain - Madhumita Murgia: Kind: captions Language: en Are you sleeping restlessly, feeling irritable or moody, forgetting little things, and feeling overwhelmed and isolated? Don't worry. We've all been there. You're probably just stressed out. Stress isn't always a bad thing. It can be handy for a burst of extra energy and focus, like when you're playing a competitive sport, or have to speak in public. But when its continuous, the kind most of us face day in and day out, it actually begins to change your brain. Chronic stress, like being overworked or having arguments at home, can affect brain size, its structure, and how it functions, right down to the level of your genes. Stress begins with something called the hypothalamus pituitary adrenal axis, a series of interactions between endocrine glands in the brain and on the kidney, which controls your body's reaction to stress. When your brain detects a stressful situation, your HPA axis is instantly activated and releases a hormone called cortisol, which primes your body for instant action. But high levels of cortisol over long periods of time wreak havoc on your brain. For example, chronic stress increases the activity level and number of neural connections in the amygdala, your brain's fear center. And as levels of cortisol rise, electric signals in your hippocampus, the part of the brain associated with learning, memories, and stress control, deteriorate. The hippocampus also inhibits the activity of the HPA axis, so when it weakens, so does your ability to control your stress. That's not all, though. Cortisol can literally cause your brain to shrink in size. Too much of it results in the loss of synaptic connections between neurons and the shrinking of your prefrontal cortex, the part of your brain the regulates behaviors like concentration, decision-making, judgement, and social interaction. It also leads to fewer new brain cells being made in the hippocampus. This means chronic stress might make it harder for you to learn and remember things, and also set the stage for more serious mental problems, like depression and eventually Alzheimer's disease. The effects of stress may filter right down to your brain's DNA. An experiment showed that the amount of nurturing a mother rat provides its newborn baby plays a part in determining how that baby responds to stress later in life. The pups of nurturing moms turned out less sensitive to stress because their brains developed more cortisol receptors, which stick to cortisol and dampen the stress response. The pups of negligent moms had the opposite outcome, and so became more sensitive to stress throughout life. These are considered epigenetic changes, meaning that they effect which genes are expressed without directly changing the genetic code. And these changes can be reversed if the moms are swapped. But there's a surprising result. The epigenetic changes caused by one single mother rat were passed down to many generations of rats after her. In other words, the results of these actions were inheritable. It's not all bad news, though. There are many ways to reverse what cortisol does to your stressed brain. The most powerful weapons are exercise and meditation, which involves breathing deeply and being aware and focused on your surroundings. Both of these activities decrease your stress and increase the size of the hippocampus, thereby improving your memory. So don't feel defeated by the pressures of daily life. Get in control of your stress before it takes control of you.

- “I Just Eat And Study": Why Indian Students Are So Stressed Out | Insight | Full Episode: Kind: captions Language: en 2024 saw an alarming statistic in India. More students took their own lives than farmers. We are losing young people to suicide. It's not only a loss to the family, but it's a loss to the community and the nation itself. Behind this is how competitive higher education has become in India. There's no holiday here in coaching. No holiday, no Sunday. It was stressful. I won't say it's not. It's almost like you either go to this best college or there will be nothing that will good that will happen in your life. In Asia, countries like China, South Korea and Singapore are commonly associated with high student stress. Is India joining their ranks? For the past one year, 22-year-old Anu Agarwal has followed a strict schedule. So I start my morning by waking up and u I have like breakfast and then I start studying. So I I study for around 7 to 8 hours a day and if possible I try to like study for more hours. I just eat and study. There's nothing else. Anu is preparing for India's Union Public Service Commission or UPSC exams. It's a standardized test required for those who wish to join the higher ranks of India's civil service. It's a grueling ordeal where candidates will eventually sit through 32 hours of examinations over the course of the year. For Anchu, D-Day draws near. This year the exam is on 25th May and um since 2 and a half months I left everyone has started preparing for it. You need at least five to six revision to like clear it. Each year across India, millions of teens and young adults sit for a myriad of tests. Year 12 students graduating from secondary education sit for the standardized all India senior school certificate examination. Then depending on which undergraduate course they wish to pursue, there are other requirements. For example, engineering hopefuls for certain programs have to take the joint entrance exam or J while those who wish to study medicine will sit for the national eligibility come entrance test or NE. And then there are profession related exams like the UPSC. For many, their exam results will determine if they can enter university or qualify for certain jobs. But all this could be taking a toll on India's students. In one survey, eight out of 10 year 9 to 12 students experience exam related anxiety. These students are between 15 to 18 years old. And for about 20 to 30% of these students, the anxiety is so severe that it impairs daily function. Being a student has always been stressful in India and uh definitely more so as and when we progress as a country or as in when new fields develop um new expectations develop new competitions arise. India has made remarkable progress in the last decade. As its industry modernized, India is now the fifth largest economy in the world and one of the fastest growing. Getting a degree is critical to climb the social and economic ladder. In 2022, there were over 43 million students in higher education, an increase of over 26% since 2015. So world over higher education has been understood or the campuses have been seen as spaces uh which ensure or which create possibility for social mobility. It creates in you abilities which would then help you cross your own social boundaries and move to other planes. Sometimes because these children are so young and impressionable, unfortunately they find themselves feeling extremely burdened by a lot of expectations that they have to hold on their shoulders. Sometimes it can be the pressure of relieving their family from a lot of uh financial concerns that the family might be having. The moment you're not a graduate, you're not looking for you. The probability of you finding a white collar job is very very little. So you have to be a graduate to start with. Um if you are wanting to get a white collar job, here's how higher education is structured in India. First are the universities. Universities are large educational institutions that offer a range of undergraduate and graduate degrees. Colleges are smaller institutions typically affiliated with universities for accreditation and focused on undergraduate studies. Some universities have multiple branches. For example, the Indian Institute of Technology has 23 branches across India. India has expanded its higher education institutions to match demand. From 600 universities in 2012, it now has more than 1,200 with some 43,000 affiliated colleges. But in the eyes of students and parents, not all schools are equal. Everybody wants just those few institutions. Everybody aspires to be uh getting into those institutions. So if you seek admissions into an engineering college, everybody wants to consider an IIT and within that an IIT Mumbai or an IIT Delhi or if you are seeking medical college admissions. So if you are in Delhi, then you want to do as or MAMC. While there is no formal definition, the consensus is that about 100 to 200 schools are considered tier 1 in India. These are schools like the Indian Institute of Technology, Indian Institute of Science, All India Institute of Medical Sciences and such. The intense competition for these tier 1 school placements is a major source of student stress. For example, IIT famously has an acceptance rate lower than Harvard or MIT in the United States. There are about 700 to 800,000 students who appear for the entrance examination with the joint J exam for engineering and about 2.2 million students who appear for uh medical admissions. It has to be really really hard. Why does this cutthroat competition happen? because we have we don't have sufficient number of seats. Why are so many millions clamoring for a spot in these handful of universities? Historically, these institutions and the students who have graduated from these institutions have done exceptionally well and the reason why they continue to do so is because they get get the best crop of students coming into those institutions. They may not necessarily have the best infrastructure, but the best students and the best jobs push themselves to or are attracted to go into those institutions. Each year, Times Higher Education publishes a list of 250 universities that produce the most employable graduates. 10 Indian schools made the 2025 list and all would be considered tier ones. there is a significant weightage to educational background especially um when you're starting to hire freshers. So that's the truth all competences apart um if two people are um evaluated together and the chances that the person who has gone through the IIT process versus somebody else who has not gone through the IT process will be preferred. In 2024, a report by talent assessment company Mercer Metal found that just about 4 in 10 Indian graduates were considered employable. The rest lack some critical skill. Students from tier 1 universities were considered the most employable while tier three the lowest. Compounding this is the high graduate unemployment rate in India. According to the International Labor Organization, in 2024, the unemployment rate among Indian graduates could have been around 29%. Compared to the general unemployment rate of almost 5%. there can be this certain pressure to pick a higher education um you know sort of stream that gives you the most money uh that gives you the most exposure that opens up the most job opportunities and I think the biggest pressure that people who are or students who are enrolling in higher education face is to make life decision at the age of 16 and 17 without even knowing the world. Uh you are expecting a person to choose how they want to exist in it. Universities have been blamed again and again for not making young people employable but that was never the aim. The objective of universities is to create independent minds critical minds uh with an ability to also introspect and criticize structures of power wherever they are. Nonetheless, millions of Indian students continue to vive for the limited seats in the top schools. And what determines their success or failure? The entrance exams. Preparing for them has spawned a multi-billion dollar industry in India. Exam coaching. [Music] [Applause] For the last 10 and 1/2 months, 18year-old Uruba Naz has lived apart from her family. Her current accommodation is a shared room at the Hinduru Academy. Travel time focus. The Hinduru Academy is a coaching center focused on national level entrance exams. It tutors both full-time students like Uruba and those that take classes after school. May Mishra a mechanical engineer by training runs the academy. We have a two shift. One is uh morning from 9 to 2 and another shift for the different student those are regular student from 4 to 7:30. Practically if I'll say we have maximum 5 hours in the morning session and uh we have 3 hours in the evening session for different students in this academy. Uh this is our fourth year and we have started from the 97 students and now we have uh in between 900 to,000 students for this current year. The exam prep industry is big business in Asia. Before the government took steps to curb it in 2021, the Chinese tutoring sector was worth some $70 billion US. In South Korea, it's over $20 billion. In India, despite its late start, the sector is burgeoning. In 2022, it was worth $6.7 billion US and is expected to grow to 15.1 billion by 2028. Talking about coaching, I think it's a necessary evil, right? So because there are tons and tons of students who appear for every every entrance examinations which you can think about and therefore there is a role which coaching uh institutions have have to play in guiding those students uh facilitating uh practice and preparation for those examinations and it's it's quite um relevant and important when I did my engineering I have completed it very smoothly but in today's scenario after seeing all the competition ation. I feel like that we need an expert can help us in all the prospects the considering the level of examination and the caution the detailed thorough study. Many of the coaching centers in India are focused on the university entrance exams. In particular, the joint entrance examination required for enrollment in tier 1 institutions like the IITs and the national institutes of technology and the national eligibility come entrance test needed for those who seek admission to medicine related courses. Others include the common university entrance test or cuit neat and J are the most popular examination nowadays. Generally we grow after listening these things. So students are also willing for getting respect. That's why they are opting for these courses. Uruba is currently studying for the NE. Anu Agural who is preparing for the civil service exam the UPSC had just wrapped up her stint at a coaching center there's no holiday here in coaching no holiday no Sunday only on festivals you get one day holiday otherwise there are no holidays now I'm done with coaching so now I prepare full-time it was stressful I won't say it's not coaching centers have become become so popular that parents are pulling their kids from state schools to attend coaching. Experts are now concerned for the toll coaching takes on young minds. Kot in Rajasthan has emerged as a coaching hub. One study here found that students who attended coaching were 13 times more likely to feel high levels of academic stress. We have co coaching uh cities where it might be a very difficult life but then there are people who are committed and who want to do this but more than that they are pressurized by their parents in the community to go into those it doesn't matter what their chances are. So um the fact is that the coaching institutions sell a few people's success and magnify it and make everybody believe that they can be successful. I do differ with that. Now our students don't sit in classrooms. Our classrooms are largely empty because those students are now coaching themselves to to be able to succeed at examination or competitive examinations which are organized by NTA. See school life is just not about scoring marks or getting admission into professional colleges. School life is also learning about give and take, friendships, life lessons, life skills. Those kids don't have that. What's more, coaching can be costly. In Kota, coaching fees can range from 40,000 to 200,000 rupees a year, roughly 460 to 2,300 US. The per capita gross national income in India is about 122,766 rupees. And there is a lot of parental pressure because so many of them you know have sold their land have fledged their jewelry and with lot of aspirations they have sent the kids to Kota. So there is this enormous pressure on the child to fulfill their parental expectations and also their expectations. So in 2024, the Ministry of Education issued guidelines to regulate the tutoring industry. Coaching centers cannot take in students younger than 16. They are also required to stop misleading advertising that promise unrealistic results. I would say that coaching centers are a symptom of the problem, but they are a very massive symptom of the problem because I feel like coaching centers also very successfully capitalize on insecurities that parents and children have and they kind of reinforce the idea that if you are determined enough and if you are hardworking enough, you will be able to achieve everything that you set your mind to. I think it is a combination of our educational system which relies so much on a onepoint examination. This uh one exam which determines your entire life is not really right and there should be opportunities for the uh youngsters to pursue what they want to do according to their capacities. But even if students ace their exams, getting into an elite university could come with a whole other set of pressures. Having cleared the university entrance exams, college life brings a different set of challenges for Indian students. The higher education India is comparatively tougher than uh other countries. The life of a student doing an undergrad or post is difficult. Well, like if I had to put it in a metaphor, I would say school felt like a fish tank whereas college felt like an aquarium. It's a scary world and you realize that you need to be resilient to survive in this world. Apart from having 7 to 8 hours of classes each day, for many this is their first time out on their own. So like used to cry and everything and just call my mom like I don't want to do this and all but now I'm this much comfortable. I don't want to go my home. When we are in environments that are so stressful, we are not operating from a space where we are feeling safe. It can sometimes um impact people at an emotional level, physical level, cognitive level where individuals can experience this much dysregulation where they start feeling burnt out. As for academic stress, burnout is especially prevalent among students on highly competitive courses. For example, in a national survey, 86% of medical students reported feeling disengaged while 80% said they faced exhaustion. There are three reasons why students in higher institutes feel stressful. One is their expectation from themselves which is which may be unrealistic. Two is the institute's expectation of what the student needs to give. The third is the parental pressure. When you set an unrealistic goal and say I have to be top of this, I have to do this. I have to do this. Then it becomes a problem. Suicide is one of the leading causes of death among youth in India and academic stress may bear a large part of the blame. According to the Indian Journal of Psychiatry, over 60% of student suicides are linked to academic stress and the fear of failure. Another study of some 8,500 students across 30 universities revealed that more than 1 in 10 students had suicidal thoughts in the past year. Over onethird of such students reportedly made the attempt. I think student suicide rates in India have always been alarming and I feel like it's only now that people are starting to talk a little bit about it. Um I think that a student specifically when they are going in these so-called competitive fields be it medicine be it engineering uh be it MBAs from the biggest business schools in the country um I think the way that these options are presented to students it's almost like a do or die situation and it is living in this constant fear that If I don't make this, I will make nothing of my life. And for students from marginalized groups, the pressure could be greater. Fear of feeling out of place. Fear of not knowing what to do next. Fear of being unable to progress at the same pace as everyone else. These were the words of Amuta Versini Sentokumar. The thirdear student at the National Law University in Delhi had written about her experience in law school. Shambvi who is pursuing a PhD at an elite institute in Delhi befriended Amuta through a student organization called collective. Amritta was a I mean very young and very uh vocal student uh law student. I remember uh meeting in joint uh programs or meetings uh on like what's happening on campus. she was very involved in these different issues on campus like around gender or cast based uh discriminations. So I got to know about uh Amruta's death through her friend who also part of collective. On the 4th of September 2024, Amruta took her own life. Amuta belonged to what's called other backward class in India, a group of communities considered socially and educationally backward. She was studying at NLU Delhi on a scholarship for marginalized groups. On campus, Amruta was an active and vocal member of the student community. So definitely a different discriminations happening in campus that was on her mind. She was herself going through. I think it was both uh yeah the academic pressure and definitely pressure of that comes with any kind of uh if not backlash but additional scrutiny on you for uh raising your voice. Data presented in parliament showed that from 2019 to 2021, a majority of students who took their own lives in central universities were from the lower casts classes and tribal communities. There are definitely some students who are more vulnerable to uh ending their life by suicide. specifically students coming from um economically marginalized backgrounds because the more marginalized a child's identity is, the more pressure the child has on its shoulders and the lesser access to resources the child has to even elevate some of the pressures that they might be going through. There have been many reports of castbased discrimination and bullying on campuses around India. John is a PhD candidate at IIT Bombay. As a graduate student, he assists in teaching. John has asked to remain anonymous. We need to open our eyes to see the discrimination and we will find that uh the discrimination is happening just in front of us. So we also see what kind of students sit in a group and what kind of students sit alone and on the back uh the back side of the classrooms and that kind of like you you will find that kind of difference of life and this difference of life is reproduced by the campus. Many people uh give the testimony that they have experienced firsthand cast based discrimination. IIT Bombay had previously rejected claims of castbased discrimination. But what makes castbased bullying common? In Indian universities, there is what is known as a reservation policy, a form of affirmative action. A certain number of seats are set aside for marginalized groups and lower casts. That is to ensure the entry of people from these sections who were not there uh 50 years back or 60 years back that has uh enriched the campuses and that has uh made the campuses very diverse. But this may have left some upperccast students feeling resentful. In an environment where top university spaces are hyper competitive, the reservation system further reduces available seats. The complaint that kota system is creating distress is a complaint made by people who come from uh communities which call themselves upperccast. The upperccast feels distressed because it sees that well everything belong to it and now uh there are different clients. Like sometimes castbased discrimination is like calling someone by a name but on other times it can also be who can cope up. So actually you shouldn't be there. It's because this policy exists that you've somehow landed up in a place that you were not fit for anyway. The issue has now caught the attention of the Supreme Court. Earlier this year, India's top judicial body had asked the University Grants Commission to provide data around castbased bullying cases across central, state, and private universities. Meanwhile, student deaths have raised concerns about mental health support on campus. About 10 years ago, the rising cases of suicides in IITs was raised in parliament. It prompted the then Ministry of Human Resource and Development to convene a task force to look into the matter. Dr. Lakshmi Vijayakumar was part of the group. She had also helped draft the first national suicide prevention strategy. Mental health support for students in higher institutes is not adequate in India at all. Every institute should have a specific mental health support plan. It should be multistakeholder. It should have students, staff and administrators. In India, every year we lose one lakh 74,000 people to suicide. We have a national suicide prevention strategy which has been formulated in 2022 but it has not been implemented which is a sad story. As for Amuta, after her passing, NLU Delhi closed the school for 2 days as an act of condolence. But before the month was out, another student would take his own life. The response since then also has been very typical of administrations everywhere which was to just uh like basically they shut down the college and please avail the counseling facilities available on campus but uh definitely no actual other changes happening on the campus. What will it take for things to change for Indian students? Dr. Lakshmi Vijayakuma founded Sneha in Chennai almost 40 years ago. It was one of the first suicide prevention organizations in India. Before long, Dr. Vijayakuma began noticing a pattern. We found that there was a 40% increase in calls from students and parents when the exam results are announced. Okay. And then we were very distressed because there were a lot of students who died because they have failed in their exams. The situation since has been bleak. In the past decade, student suicides in India have grown at an average of 4% per year. This prompted a call for change by Indian media last year. I really want to ask ourselves this question that what does really change mean in this particular context? Who really needs to change? H is it the parents that needs to change? Are is it the government policies that need to change? Is it the coaching classes that needs to change? Is it the institute that needs to change as well? Because I feel like this is a problem that is so multifaceted and had so many layers that every stakeholder in this situation needs to consider this as a problem for them to even think of changing it. One solution may be to increase the number of tier 1 schools and by doing so raise the acceptance rate. We have over uh 40,000 plus about 42,000 plus colleges about 1200 plus universities in our country. But uh the fact is that we large number of institutions exist in our country who are very good uh but they may not be ranked or they may not be perceived as the absolute best because of the fact that they they're not as old. They are not delivered as many people to the industry yet but wait I think those guys will catch up. There'll be more and more institutions who will stand up and say we can rub shoulders with the absolute best. However, India's universities struggle with faculty recruitment. In a recent parliamentary report, elite institutions face an acute professor shortage, some with more than 50% vacancies. If you look at uh state universities, uh they are in very empowered state. Thousands and thousands of teaching positions vacant. So teaching positions have been contextualized. As a result, there is an issue of teaching quality. In 2019, the IIT Council, the institute's own governing body, found that more than half of its professors did not put in enough effort on research and teaching. In last 10 years, most of the central universities have been packed uh with teachers who just cannot teach. They're incompetent. They're inefficient. Uh they're not trained in their disciplines. To solve this, IIT would adopt a tenure track for professors. New academic staff were put on a 5-year probationary period. Tenure would be offered based on performance. The probationary period had previously been one year. Several other universities would follow this practice. UGC is trying its best to check the quality. They should go to check the teachers. Teachers are underpaid. So teacher should be given proper facilities. They are talent should be respected. So the teacher should be well paid, well selected on the basis of talent. There were also calls to improve mental health awareness and support on campus. In 2022, the University Grants Commission issued guidelines to all central and state universities to promote mental health and student well-being. For one, all higher educational institutes are required to have a student services center to address stress and emotional needs. But the UGC acknowledged the shortage of mental health professionals in India. So there is one counselor for now more than 8,000 students. When I was a student, there were around 8,000. I think having more counselors is definitely helpful but it's not going to eradicate mental health problems in a child because mental health concerns that a student opting for higher education um is going through is not necessarily going through because they don't have the capacity to deal with certain emotions. It's also because structurally they are in a position in a world that expects so much of them uh where they cannot understand how to navigate this space as well. The UGC's guidelines came out of India's move to overhaul its education sector outlined in 2020's national education policy or NEP. The NEP included goals like more equitable education, improving teaching quality and focusing on holistic and multidisciplinary learning. Now there is no barrier like teams that child can select the subject according to the choice can go for uh more science subjects or uh be with music and be with languages and whatever you like. So it is a good thing that the basic talent of child will not be killed. NEP I think is one of the most transformational policies which uh the government of India has brought in starting from primary education to the the absolute higher education everything every aspect of that has been looked at and it is not that it is cast in stone there are many reviews which are happening and they upgrading it constantly. It brings in so much of opportunities for the student. It brings in so many opportunities for the institutions to deliver better quality better with greater amount of flexibility. It lacks direction and it's not written in a policy in the language of a policy document. uh you will find the word quality being mentioned more than 50 times or even more in in the document. But what constitutes quality? How do you ensure quality? When do you say autonomy? What does autonomy mean? One stated aim of the NEP is to change the exam focused culture and eliminate the need for coaching. It proposes to do so with regular assessments rather than the current system of one major final exam. In Tamil Nadu, Dr. Vijayakuma and her organization had shown that diminishing the gravity of assessments reduces student stress. 20 years ago, they petitioned the state government to give students a doover on their exams. See, it's not fair for a young person who has failed in one or two subjects by a few marks to lose an entire academic year and be the butt of ridicule and be stigmatized by by people around. give them a hope, have another exam a month later or two months later so that they catch up because anyway the uh the school starts after a gap of 3 months. In 2004, Tamil Nadu allowed class 10 and 12 students to retake their exams and since then Tamil Nadu's exam failure suicides have reportedly dropped by 70%. Several other states have adopted the same practice. The step of seeing that we don't have students dying because they have failed in the exams is crucial because it's not only a loss to the family but it's a loss to the community and the nation itself because they are all young people who were the backbone of any nation. Actually this is the time we are implementing NEP 2020 and it is very early stage. After five six years the review will come out and things will be in the public domain and we will try to improve improvise and come up with the uh makeup of those things. But perhaps the change that is needed must happen in Indian society at large. Last year a tech CEO said employees should be willing to work 14 hours 6 days a week. This prompted a wide discussion about India's hustle culture. Somehow we have believed that if an employee is working longer hours the employee is being more productive and somehow we have equated just giving your life to work as dedication. I don't think we're ready to prioritize student stress in India because we have normalized it to that degree that if you've chosen this you have to study 14 hours a day. You have to give all your life here. And as the NE exam draws near, these are the hours prepared to pull. 10 to 12 hours. With so much attention now given to student stress, where is education in India headed? This March, the Supreme Court issued significant directions to address the prevention of suicides in higher education. Will this be the catalyst for change? Quality education should be every child's right. Uh and no child should compete to want to study. I really hope in 10 years time Indian India's higher education sector remains about education and not about business and that's my hope. Higher education in India is in a growing pace and the induction of uh new disciplines matchic with the changing challenges and scenario of the whole world. The Indian higher education system is also trying to match up with all those things. Where do I see higher education in in India in 10 years time will be the world's number one. The number of research institutions the number of institutions putting focus on research is multiplying by the day. So every which way I look at it both in terms of quality quantity um the depth the width of the higher education system is going to be the best in the world.
